Abstract: Egocentric Visual Question Answering (VQA) has attracted widespread attention as an important task for enabling Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to interact with the real world. However, existing MLLMs struggle to perform effective spatial reasoning in complex egocentric scenes due to their limited spatial perception capabilities. To this end, we introduce Ego Scene Augmentation (ESA), an egocentric spatial perception framework, which actively enhances the spatial perception capabilities from the egocentric perspective, powered by the proposed Ego-element Graph. Our core insight is leveraging the Ego-element Graph as an intermediary representation to augment the egocentric spatial perception of MLLMs via visual foundational models. Specifically, we 1) construct the Ego-element Graph, which encapsulates and integrates egocentric spatial features enabled by visual foundational models; 2) enhance the spatial perception capabilities of MLLMs via the Ego-element Graph for ego-perspective scenes. Our proposed ESA framework presents significant performance improvement on the EgoTextVQA benchmark. We achieve an 8.14% gain on the indoor setting and an 8.72% gain on the outdoor setting. Furthermore, our ESA shows the most impressive performance improvement in the shopping subset of the indoor setting. The project code is publicly available.

</details>


### [170] [Multi-Scale ViT Inference with Habitat-Fit Priors and kNN Retrieval for Multi-Species Plant Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14509)
*Alper Erten,Murilo Gustineli,Adrian Cheung*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DS@GT ARC developed a pipeline for multi-species plant identification for PlantCLEF 2026, combining DINOv2 ViT-L/14 classification, tile decomposition, FAISS kNN post-processing, and geographic priors, achieving third place with macro-F1 score of 0.43902 on the private leaderboard.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to tackle the challenge of identifying multiple plant species from high-resolution images, despite having training limited to single-label images. This involves advancing techniques for ecological monitoring in complex scenarios.

Method: The approach utilizes DINOv2 ViT-L/14 applied on multi-scale tiles, integrated with FAISS kNN for post-processing, habitat-fit demotion, geographic masking, and temporal fusion. Other experimental methods, like cross-region transformers and synthetic quadrats, were explored but proved ineffective.

Result: The third-place solution achieved a macro-F1 score of 0.43902 on private leaderboard (public score: 0.51096). Certain methods significantly contributed, while others like instance-aware segmentation and synthetic pseudo-quadrats yielded null results.

Conclusion: The pipeline's success highlights the importance of creative post-processing techniques and geospatial priors in complex ecological data analysis. Future directions could explore enhancements such as more effective training-centric methods.

Abstract: This paper describes DS@GT ARC's third-place solution to the PlantCLEF 2026 challenge on multi-species plant identification in vegetation quadrat images, where systems must predict every species present in high-resolution (~3000 x 3000 pixel) plot photographs while training only on single-label images of individual plants. The pipeline is built around a fine-tuned DINOv2 ViT-L/14 classifier applied over a multi-scale tile decomposition of each quadrat, with per-tile predictions blended with a FAISS kNN retriever and post-processed by source-aware temporal fusion across repeated plot visits, a habitat-fit demotion that injects geographic and altitude priors from the training data, and a South-Western Europe geographic mask. Habitat-fit demotion and multi-scale aggregation are the largest individual contributors in the ablations. Two complementary training-centric directions, a cross-region transformer with noisy-student distillation on the LUCAS dataset and a label-as-query transformer decoder over synthetic CLS-domain pseudo-quadrats, yielded null results. An inference-time augmentation with instance-aware segmentation crops also did not improve performance. The selected submission reaches a private-leaderboard macro-F1 of 0.43902 (third place; public 0.51096); an unselected configuration of the same pipeline scored above 0.45 on the private set. Code: https://github.com/dsgt-arc/plantclef-2026.

</details>


### [171] [Compression of 3D Gaussian Splatting Data Using GPU-friendly Graphics Texture Coding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14513)
*Amir Said,Randall Rauwendaal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces a GPU-driven method to compress spherical harmonic coefficients for rendering 3D scenes, achieving efficient parallel decoding and high visual quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the large memory requirements in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) due to spherical harmonic coefficients, utilizing GPU capabilities for compression.

Method: The authors use texture compression schemes like BC1 and BC7 to compress color coefficients, optimize local grouping and reordering of primitives, and implement a bit-rate control strategy.

Result: Their approach demonstrates efficient GPU-based decompression with minimal quality degradation for 3D rendered scenes.

Conclusion: Using hardware-accelerated texture formats for SH compression delivers highly efficient rendering while controlling memory usage and preserving visual quality.

Abstract: Techniques for modeling 3D scenes from image collections, such as 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS), are capable of generating high-quality novel views by leveraging graphics primitives with view-dependent appearance. In 3DGS, spherical harmonic (SH) are employed to model view-dependent color, resulting in a large number of SH coefficients per primitive and large memory requirements. While compression approaches have been proposed to mitigate this problem, they do not exploit the capabilities of modern Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) for parallel decoding and rendering. In this paper, we propose a method for compressing SH color coefficients using texture compression schemes specifically designed for efficient parallel GPU decoding and supported by dedicated hardware acceleration. It is shown that those methods can compress color coefficients more effectively than 2D textures by exploiting the fact that primitives can be locally grouped and reordered according to color. Furthermore, we introduce a bit-rate control strategy that preserves random access, enabling large-scale parallelization without compromising rendering performance. Experimental results using BC1 and BC7 texture compression formats show that GPU-based decompression can be achieved with negligible or imperceptible degradation in the visual quality of rendered 3DGS scenes.

</details>


### [172] [VTM-Nav: Hierarchical Visual-Topological Memory for Cross-Episode Object-Goal Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14514)
*Xiaoran Xu,Yupeng Wu,Tianyu Xue,Yifan Xu,Xuanran Dong,Xiaoshan Yang,Changsheng Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents a navigation framework for embodied agents called \method, which uses structured visual-topological memory to enhance object-goal navigation performance in the same scene across episodes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations in episodic navigation protocols, which reset an agent's state after each session, the authors aim to leverage persistent experience reuse for object-goal navigation using vision-language models.

Method: The framework, \method, incorporates a hierarchical Visual-Topological Memory (VTM) that organizes scene knowledge at room and object levels for experience reuse. It uses coarse-to-fine memory matching combined with observational agreement to guide navigation.

Result: \method outperforms a strengthened baseline (WMNav) on three benchmarks, showcasing improved navigation performance and robustness in repeated same-scene scenarios.

Conclusion: Structured visual-topological memory is effective for enhancing object-goal navigation, enabling embodied agents to reuse experience persistently across episodes without retraining or parameter updates.

Abstract: Object-goal navigation requires an embodied agent to locate and reach an instance of a specified object category in an indoor environment. Recent training-free approaches leverage vision-language models (VLMs) for open-vocabulary semantic reasoning, but are typically evaluated under an episodic protocol that resets all scene-specific state after each episode. We introduce Cross-Episode Object-Goal Navigation, in which an agent repeatedly operates in the same scene, retains only self-acquired experience, and keeps its model parameters fixed. To support experience reuse, we present \method, a training-free VLM navigation framework with a persistent hierarchical Visual-Topological Memory (VTM). The VTM organizes scene knowledge at room and object levels and retrieves relevant experience through coarse-to-fine matching, providing memory as soft guidance only when it agrees with current observations. A conservative execution guard further mitigates oscillations, blocked motions, and premature stopping. Under a controlled same-scene protocol, we evaluate \method{} on three benchmarks, HM3D v0.1, HM3D v0.2, and MP3D, and compare it with a strengthened WMNav baseline augmented with cross-episode textual memory, while keeping the VLM backbone and action pipeline identical. \method{} achieves the best performance across all three benchmarks, demonstrating the effectiveness and robustness of structured visual-topological experience reuse across datasets.

</details>


### [173] [Uni-AdaVD: Universal Concept Erasure for Visual Generation via Orthogonal Value Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14521)
*Qifan Zhou,Yuan Wang,Yanbin Hao,Xiang Wang,Kuien Liu,Richang Hong,Meng Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Uni-AdaVD is a universal inference-time framework for removing unwanted concepts from visual generative models while preserving desired content and original model weights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of undesirable concept absorption by visual generative models due to uncurated pretraining data, making it critical to ensure their safe deployment.

Method: Uni-AdaVD utilizes the multimodal attention value space for intervention, innovative encoder-aware target representation to pinpoint semantics, and combines orthogonal value decomposition with adaptive shifts for concept erasure without modifying original model weights.

Result: The framework was tested on diverse architectures, including U-Net, DiT, autoregressive image generators, and text-to-video models, showing strong performance in erasing single and multi-concepts while retaining generative priors.

Conclusion: Uni-AdaVD offers a novel and efficient safety mechanism for visual generative models, enabling the removal of specific concepts while maintaining the integrity of other generation capabilities.

Abstract: Visual generative models inevitably absorb undesirable concepts from uncurated pretraining data, making concept erasure essential for safe deployment. Existing erasure methods, however, are often architecture-specific and struggle to remove target concepts while preserving non-target content and generative priors. We present Uni-AdaVD, a universal inference-time concept erasure framework for visual generation. Uni-AdaVD treats the value space of multimodal attention as a unified intervention space and introduces encoder-aware target representation construction to localize target semantics across heterogeneous text encoders. It further combines orthogonal value decomposition with an adaptive erasing shift to suppress target semantic directions without updating the original model weights. Extensive experiments on U-Net-, DiT-, and autoregressive image generators, as well as text-to-video models, demonstrate strong performance on single- and multi-concept erasure while preserving non-target priors. These results suggest that Uni-AdaVD provides an efficient and adaptable safety mechanism for modern visual generative models. Our code is available at https://github.com/QifanZhou/Uni-AdaVD.

</details>


### [174] [SwinAD: Multi-stage feature reconstruction for unsupervised industrial anomaly detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14534)
*Huong Ninh,Chien Thai,Mai Xuan Trang,Vu-Minh Le,Thanh Ha Le,Long Tran*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces SwinAD, a framework that improves multi-class unsupervised industrial anomaly detection using Swin Transformer V2 and diverse multi-scale reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of detecting industrial anomalies in multi-class settings where diverse normal patterns make anomaly discrimination difficult.

Method: SwinAD utilizes a frozen pretrained Swin Transformer V2 encoder, a feature-diversity preserving decoder, and stage-wise bottleneck modules with dropout for robust normal pattern reconstruction. It aggregates discrepancies between encoder features and reconstructed features across multiple scales to generate anomaly maps.

Result: SwinAD demonstrates competitive image-level performance and superior pixel-level localization accuracy on benchmarks like MVTec AD, VisA, and Real-IAD, showing marked improvements in pixel-level metrics on MVTec AD.

Conclusion: The hierarchical and diverse multi-scale reconstruction approach in SwinAD effectively enhances anomaly localization in complex multi-class scenarios, making it a strong unsupervised detection framework.

Abstract: Industrial anomaly detection aims to identify and localize defective regions without relying on exhaustive annotations of all possible defect types. Although recent unsupervised methods have achieved strong performance, most are primarily designed for single-class settings and often struggle in multi-class scenarios, where diverse normal patterns may lead to over-generalization and reduce the discriminative capability between normal and anomalous regions. In this paper, we propose SwinAD, a reconstruction-based framework for multi-class unsupervised anomaly detection that leverages a frozen pretrained Swin Transformer V2 encoder and a feature diversity-preserving reconstruction decoder. The hierarchical encoder provides semantically rich multi-scale features, while stage-wise bottleneck modules with dropout prevent trivial identity mapping and encourage robust reconstruction of normal patterns. To further improve localization, we introduce a feature diversity-preserving reconstruction framework that maintains complementary reconstruction hypotheses instead of relying on a single decoding branch. The discrepancies between encoder features and the two reconstructed features are then aggregated across multiple scales to produce the final anomaly map. Experiments conducted on three industrial anomaly detection benchmarks, including MVTec AD, VisA, and Real-IAD, demonstrate that SwinAD achieves competitive image-level performance and strong pixel-level localization accuracy, with particularly notable improvements in pixel-level AP and 1 on MVTec AD. These results indicate that combining hierarchical Swin features with diverse multi-scale reconstruction substantially improve pixel-level localization in multi-class unsupervised anomaly setting.

</details>


### [175] [3D Geometric Tooth Alignment Planning via Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14544)
*Yong Li,Jianwen Lou,Jiayue Ma,Yao-Xiang Ding,Youyi Zheng,Haihua Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) framework aimed at automating 3D geometric tooth alignment planning, optimizing trajectory safety and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to improve modern digital orthodontics by automating the 3D tooth alignment process using advanced algorithms and reducing reliance on manual trajectory planning.

Method: The authors propose a framework utilizing Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) enhanced with a Transformer-based agent, dynamic masking for spatial constraints, and curriculum learning strategies.

Result: The method was tested on a dataset of 10K clinical treatment plans, showing better path safety and geometric efficiency compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: The study provides a robust and effective automated solution for improving trajectory planning in orthodontics, advancing clinical practice and treatment efficiency.

Abstract: 3D geometric tooth alignment planning, which determines sequential trajectories from initial malocclusion to the final target alignment, is a cornerstone of modern digital orthodontics. This paper presents a novel deep reinforcement learning (DRL) framework to automate the generation of these alignment paths. We formulate the planning process as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) to capture its sequential decision-making nature, focusing on optimizing geometric trajectories while integrating essential spatial constraints, such as inter-dental collision avoidance and path efficiency. The proposed method leverages the Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) algorithm, enhanced by three key innovations: (1) a Transformer-based agent to model complex spatial interactions between teeth and manage high-dimensional state-action spaces; (2) a dynamic masking scheme that restricts movement to a sparse subset of teeth per step, better reflecting the clinical logic of sequential alignment; and (3) a two-stage curriculum learning strategy that gradually increases task difficulty to ensure training stability and efficient path discovery. We evaluate our approach on a dataset of 10K expert-designed treatment plans based on clinical data. Experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms existing baselines in terms of path safety and geometric efficiency, providing a robust and automated solution for 3D geometric orthodontic alignment planning.

</details>


### [176] [AdaTurn: Budget-Aware Test-Time Scaling for Active Visual Perception Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14547)
*Susan Liang,Chao Huang,Filippos Bellos,Jing Bi,Jason J Corso,Chenliang Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes AdaTurn, a framework to improve the performance of active visual agents under varying rollout budgets by making them budget-aware and teaching them to handle limited-turn scenarios effectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation of this paper is to address the issue of 'catastrophic truncation,' where active visual agents fail to provide valid answers when deployment-time rollout budgets are smaller than their preferred trajectories.

Method: The authors introduce AdaTurn, a framework that conditions the agent on allowed turns, incorporates Forced-Answer DAPO (FA-DAPO) to train boundary behavior under budget constraints, and uses randomized budgets during training and inference alongside a load-balanced scheduler.

Result: AdaTurn significantly improves low-budget accuracy, such as raising VisualProbe-Medium performance from 36.7% to 47.6% at four turns. It also preserves strong scalability at larger budgets and transfers effectively to different backbones and benchmarks.

Conclusion: AdaTurn addresses the challenges of limited deployment budgets by training agents in a budget-aware manner, thereby improving their performance under tight constraints while maintaining scalability for larger budgets.

Abstract: Active visual agents solve fine-grained image tasks by interleaving reasoning with image-grounding actions across multiple turns. However, deployment-time rollout budgets are rarely fixed: some requests permit long rollouts, while others require the agent to act under a tight turn limit. Existing methods train the policy as if the rollout budget were hidden, so when the available budget is smaller than the trajectory the agent prefers, the interaction is often truncated before any valid answer is produced; we term this failure \emph{catastrophic truncation}. To overcome this challenge, we present AdaTurn, a budget-aware framework that conditions the agent on the allowed number of turns and explicitly trains the boundary behavior induced by the budget. Our key component, Forced-Answer DAPO (FA-DAPO), converts the over-budget event from a masked or penalized failure into a trainable final-decision step, teaching the model to synthesize partial evidence when further tool use is no longer possible. We further randomize rollout budgets during both training and inference and introduce a load-balanced scheduler that makes such operations practical. AdaTurn substantially improves low-budget accuracy, for example raising VisualProbe-Medium from 36.7% to 47.6% at four turns, while preserving strong scaling at larger budgets and transferring effectively to multiple backbones and general multimodal benchmarks.

</details>


### [177] [HyMobileAgent: Data-Environment Co-Scaling for Efficient GUI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14548)
*Hy Vision Team,Huawen Shen,Zhengyang Tang,Shangpin Peng,Liang Wu,Anran Zhang,Weinong Wang,Yiduo Guo,Chenxin Li,Zhengyao Fang,Yang Ding,Junyi Li,Fei Tang,Zheng Ruan,Yi Zhang,Xingran Zhou,Dingchen Yang,Sunqi Fan,Zhiyi Wan,Han Hu,Xin Lai,Pengyuan Lyu,Chengquan Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HyMobileAgent introduces a new framework combining GUI perception and training environments for mobile GUI agents built on the Hy3.0-VL-A3B vision-native model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop mobile agents capable of interacting precisely and reliably with complex mobile GUI environments, which is critical for advancing digital embodied intelligence.

Method: HyMobileAgent integrates GUI perception methodologies, structured knowledge pipelines, large-scale action data pipelines, a resettable training environment (PhoneWorld Mock App Factory), and Planning-and-Reflection mechanisms alongside a progressive training recipe.

Result: This approach provides scalable, high-quality interaction data and robust long-horizon decision-making capabilities under execution errors, addressing bottlenecks in mobile interaction.

Conclusion: The HyMobileAgent framework enhances mobile agent capabilities by combining model scaling with innovative data and environment scaling methodologies for improved mobile interaction performance.

Abstract: As large multimodal models move from understanding content to operating on digital environments, mobile GUI has emerged as a challenging and consequential testbed for digital embodied intelligence. Mobile agents operate under three coupled constraints: precise perception of complex interfaces, scalable acquisition of high-quality interaction data, and robust long-horizon decision making under compounding execution errors. This report presents HyMobileAgent, a mobile GUI agent built on Hy3.0-VL-A3B, a vision-native foundation model featuring native any-resolution input, an A3B-scale deployment budget, and a 32K context window to model extended interaction histories. Rather than relying solely on model scaling, we develop a joint data and environment centric scaling framework to address the key bottlenecks of mobile interaction.
  Our framework integrates a GUI perception flywheel combining mock-interface synthesis, rejection sampling, and icon-specific augmentation; a knowledge pipeline that transforms tutorial videos into structured interaction data; a million-scale action data pipeline deployed across more than 2000 sandbox and real-device instances with automated failure attribution; the PhoneWorld Mock App Factory, providing a resettable training environment with 34 mock applications and over 34000 tasks; and a structured Planning-and-Reflection mechanism with explicit dead-loop detection for reliable long-horizon execution.
  We also introduce a progressive training recipe consisting of mid-training, supervised fine-tuning, and reinforcement learning with task-specific reward designs.

</details>


### [178] [Breaking the Model Forgetting Cycle in Long-Incremental 3D Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14560)
*Peisheng Qian,Jie Xu,Xulei Yang,Na Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes the Learning-Dynamics-driven Memory and Review (LDMR) framework to alleviate model forgetting in incremental 3D object detection, outperforming baselines in long-incremental scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the severe model forgetting issue in incremental 3D object detection, especially in long-incremental sequences, that arises from data distribution shifts and accumulated pseudo-labeling errors.

Method: The proposed LDMR framework includes two mechanisms: (i) intra-stage review that divides training into sub-stages, focusing on remembering forgotten objects, and (ii) cross-stage memory evolution that uses a memory bank to adapt knowledge transfer while considering scene learnability and diversity.

Result: LDMR effectively mitigates model forgetting and consistently surpasses baseline methods in extensive experiments on benchmarks SUN RGB-D and ScanNetV2, particularly under long-incremental protocols.

Conclusion: The LDMR framework demonstrates a significant improvement in long-term incremental 3D object detection by addressing data distribution shifts and enhancing memory retention through human-inspired mechanisms like review and memory evolution.

Abstract: Incremental 3D object detection requires a detector to learn novel object classes while remembering previously learned ones over sequentially arriving data. Previous methods, primarily based on pseudo-labeling, perform reasonably in short-incremental stages but still suffer from severe model forgetting when dealing with long-incremental sequences. We investigate this failure and reveal a detrimental self-reinforcing cycle: data distribution shift of novel classes causes model forgetting on old classes, which further produces accumulated error in pseudo-labeling that exacerbates model degradation. To address this issue, we draw inspiration from the human learning process and propose the \emph{Learning-Dynamics-driven Memory and Review} (LDMR) framework. LDMR monitors per-class detection quality at periodic training checkpoints and uses these learning-dynamics signals to drive two innovative mechanisms, namely (i) human-like intra-stage review that divides each incremental stage into multiple sub-stages' training and concentrates on remembering the most-forgotten objects, and (ii) scene-aware cross-stage memory evolution that evolves a memory bank to transfer knowledge between two consecutive stages by jointly considering scene learnability and diversity. Extensive experiments across multiple long-incremental protocols on indoor benchmarks SUN RGB-D and ScanNetV2 show that LDMR substantially mitigates the model forgetting and outperforms all baselines by a clear margin. Code is available at https://github.com/qianpeisheng/LDMR.

</details>


### [179] [Advanced Image Generation: Negative Prompt Optimization and Latent Classifier Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14580)
*Vaddi Charan Sai Nandan Reddy,Harini B,Chandana M S*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces a new system combining negative prompt optimization and latent-space classifier guidance to enhance image generation in Stable Diffusion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve the quality and semantic fidelity of images generated by Stable Diffusion, addressing issues of low image quality and artifacts.

Method: Utilizing a fine-tuned sequence-to-sequence language model for negative prompt optimization, coupled with a CNN-RNN classifier to guide diffusion steps and rollback low-quality generative updates.

Result: The dual-guidance framework led to reduced artifacts and enhanced semantic fidelity in generated images compared to baseline methods.

Conclusion: The proposed system successfully integrates novel techniques to improve image generation, demonstrating its effectiveness and significance in the diffusion-based generative models.

Abstract: We present a novel system that integrates negative prompt optimization via a fine-tuned sequence-to-sequence LLM and latent-space classifier guidance to improve the quality of images generated by Stable Diffusion. Our approach automatically generates optimized negative prompts, and employs a CNN-RNN hybrid classifier to evaluate and guide diffusion steps, rolling back low-quality latent updates. Experimental results demonstrate that our dual-guidance framework reduces artifacts and improves semantic fidelity compared to baseline diffusion.

</details>


### [180] [MagicPrompt: Ultra-Lightweight Prompt Tuning for Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14595)
*Yinhan Zhang,Dinwei Tan,Xianghao Kong,Yue Ma,Yeying Jin,Anyi Rao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MagicPrompt is a lightweight framework for fine-tuning billion-scale video diffusion models that achieves high efficiency by using less than 1% trainable parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the high computational costs of fine-tuning large-scale video diffusion models and address issues in existing PEFT methods, like substantial trainable parameters and instability in reward-based training.

Method: Combines Attention-Embedded Prompt Tuning for steering generation with soft prompts and Dual-Space Reward Feedback Optimization for stable condition-guided reward training.

Result: Experiments demonstrate competitive performance, substantial reduction in training costs, and extreme parameter efficiency.

Conclusion: MagicPrompt resolves critical flaws in PEFT methods, provides stable optimization, and delivers an efficient approach for fine-tuning large-scale VDMs.

Abstract: Large-scale video diffusion models (VDMs) deliver strong generation performance, but full fine-tuning for downstream tasks incurs prohibitive computational costs. Existing parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods have two critical flaws on billion-scale models: they still require substantial trainable parameters, and reward-based training suffers from noise-induced optimization instability in condition-guided tasks. We propose MagicPrompt, a lightweight framework that achieves extreme parameter efficiency and stable reward optimization. It first adopts Attention-Embedded Prompt Tuning, which steers generation via lightweight soft prompts with orders of magnitude fewer parameters while preserving pre-trained knowledge. It further introduces Dual-Space Reward Feedback Optimization, which uses self-supervised latent objectives to improve condition-guided reward training. Experiments show MagicPrompt reaches competitive performance with less than 1\% trainable parameters and notably reduces training costs.

</details>


### [181] [Hough-SIFT: Robust Image Registration for Linear Structures via Hough Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14598)
*Masaki Satoh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Hough-SIFT enhances image registration by matching SIFT descriptors in the Hough space, improving robustness in scenes with strong linear structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the shortcomings of SIFT in handling scenes with linear structures, as local features become ambiguous in such cases.

Method: The authors introduce Hough-SIFT, which matches SIFT descriptors in the Hough space. This approach exploits distinctive peaks in linear structures to improve descriptor discriminability.

Result: Experiments show Hough-SIFT is robust in linear scenes where SIFT performs poorly, without compromising accuracy in normal scenes.

Conclusion: Hough-SIFT effectively resolves the limitations of traditional SIFT methods in challenging linear scenes while maintaining overall accuracy.

Abstract: Image registration is essential in applications such as electronic image stabilization. Scale-Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT), a widely used local keypoint detector and descriptor, typically provides accurate registration; however, it often fails in scenes with strong linear structures (e.g., shutters), where local features become ambiguous. We propose Hough-SIFT, a robust registration method that performs SIFT descriptor matching in Hough space. In this domain, linear structures form distinctive peaks that restore descriptor discriminability. Experiments demonstrate that Hough-SIFT is robust in linear scenes where SIFT frequently fails, while maintaining accuracy comparable to SIFT in normal scenes.

</details>


### [182] [Knowing You at First Glance: Inferring Apparent Personality from Faces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14631)
*Shuhuan Chen,Xiangyu Zhu,Weisong Zhao,Haichao Shi,Xiao-Yu Zhang,Zhen Lei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces GlanceFace, an AI framework for inferring perceived personality (MBTI type) from facial images using vision-language models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore whether facial cues alone can infer perceived personality traits, focusing on MBTI types to aid applications like human-robot interaction.

Method: The approach combines vision-language models for semantic priors and a facial representation module to capture personality cues, using uncertainty-aware learning for noisy annotations.

Result: Experiments show strong prediction performance on MBTI personality benchmarks and identify links between facial features and perceived traits.

Conclusion: GlanceFace shows promise in supporting adaptive interaction strategies for social agents, backed by validated results and publicly shared resources.

Abstract: Inferring apparent personality from facial images is important in social scenarios for embodied agents in human-robot interaction. Unlike inferring intrinsic personality traits via conversation, this task models first-impression personality perception based solely on facial appearance before interaction begins. Existing studies mainly focus on the Big Five personality model and often rely on language or multimodal inputs. As a result, it remains unclear whether facial cues alone can support meaningful associations with perceived personality traits. This question is particularly relevant for MBTI types, which are widely used in practice and more readily interpretable by large language models. To this end, we propose \textbf{GlanceFace}, an end-to-end framework for apparent personality inference leveraging vision-language models to introduce semantic priors and a semantic-enhanced facial representation module to capture subtle personality-related cues, together with an uncertainty-aware learning strategy to handle noisy and subjective annotations. Extensive experiments demonstrate strong performance on MBTI-based apparent personality benchmarks and reveal relationships between facial characteristics and perceived personality traits, highlighting its potential to support adaptive initial interaction strategies for embodied agents. The code and dataset are available at https://github.com/MrHuan3/GlanceFace.

</details>


### [183] [Autoregressive Modeling of Film with Applications in Video Montage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14645)
*Marcelo Sandoval-Castañeda,Fabian Caba Heilbron,Shiry Ginosar,Bryan Rusell,Josef Sivic,Alexei A. Efros,Greg Shakhnarovich*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FilmGPT is a transformer model aimed at improving video montage by selecting shots based on implicit film grammar learned from movie data, without generating new frames.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of converting unorganized, raw video footage into coherent cinematic sequences by leveraging data-driven patterns obtained from movies.

Method: FilmGPT employs a long-context autoregressive transformer that learns film grammar implicitly from a movie dataset. It uses footage-constrained decoding to select the next shot during inference.

Result: FilmGPT outperformed previous state-of-the-art methods in shot sequence ordering, and user studies showed significant improvements in film editing tasks using FilmGPT.

Conclusion: FilmGPT applies data-driven film grammar for video montage, enabling enhanced editing techniques across a variety of applications, such as automatic video trimming and human-in-the-loop editing.

Abstract: This work introduces FilmGPT, an autoregressive transformer designed to address the challenge of video montage--turning a collection of raw, "unwatchable" footage into coherent cinematic sequences. Inspired by language learning in modern LLMs, we train a long-context autoregressive transformer on a large corpus of movies. The aim is to implicitly capture the "grammar" of film directly from data rather than from hand-coded rules. Unlike other generative models, FilmGPT does not generate any new video frames. Instead, at inference time, we introduce a footage-constrained decoding algorithm to select the best next shot from the input raw footage according to the statistical patterns learned from films. We first evaluate these learned statistics directly by using the FilmGPT autoregressive model for next shot prediction on a standard benchmark of shot sequence ordering, outperforming the previous state of the art. We then evaluate our footage-constrained decoding algorithm on the full film editing task via a user study, and find that our FilmGPT-based editing significantly outperforms previous approaches. Finally, we demonstrate the applicability of FilmGPT to a wide range of applications in video montage, from automatic video segment trimming to human-in-the-loop film editing.

</details>


### [184] [VIABench: A Comprehensive Video Benchmark Collected from Blind Individuals for Visual Impairment Assistance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14660)
*Yunfeng Liu,Yuandong Yang,Jiarui Han,Zhenpeng Huang,Yuqing Tang,Xiangyu Zeng,Gangshan Wu,Limin Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces VIABench, a video benchmark designed to evaluate multimodal large language models (MLLMs) in assisting visually impaired individuals (VIIs), focusing on core tasks like proactive reminders, visual question answering, and vision-guided interaction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visually impaired individuals face daily challenges due to limited access to visual information, which remains inadequately addressed by existing MLLMs.

Method: VIABench uses first-person videos from VIIs and benchmarks MLLMs across three tasks—learning to provide proactive navigation reminders, answer visual questions, and facilitate vision-guided interactions, with support for online and offline evaluation.

Result: Current MLLMs show significant limitations, particularly in the proactive reminder task requiring anticipation and real-time response.

Conclusion: VIABench aims to guide research towards developing specialized MLLMs for enhancing navigation and interaction for visually impaired individuals.

Abstract: Visually impaired individuals (VIIs) encounter significant daily challenges due to limited access to visual information. Although Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved impressive results on general vision and language tasks, their practical utility in real-world blind assistance still remains largely underexplored. To fill this gap, we introduce VIABench, a comprehensive video benchmark specifically designed to evaluate MLLMs in Visually Impaired Assistance scenarios using first-person videos recorded or shared by VIIs themselves. VIABench defines three core tasks, each targeting a distinct requirement in visual assistance. Proactive Reminder: Assesses the model's ability to interpret ongoing video content while proactively anticipating and verbally describing upcoming navigation-critical events; Visual Question Answering (VQA): Evaluates the model's capacity to answer user-posed questions about the environment or objects within the video; Vision-Guided Interaction: Tests context-aware reasoning to accomplish intentional interactions between user and environment. To ensure a robust and fair evaluation, we propose a rigorous benchmarking pipeline that supports both online (real-time) and offline settings. Our experiments demonstrate that current MLLMs still struggle to deliver comprehensive support for VIIs, especially in the Proactive Reminder task, which demands accurate anticipation and real-time responsiveness. We hope VIABench will drive future research toward developing customized MLLMs for real-world assistance, ultimately improving navigation and interaction experiences for visually impaired individuals. Code and data will be released at https://github.com/MCG-NJU/VIABench.

</details>


### [185] [ReBind: Multi-Reference Video Editing via Structured Instructions with Explicit Reference Relationships](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14681)
*Xinyu Liu,Shihao Li,Weihong Lin,Xinlong Chen,Yang Shi,Yujin Han,Yiyang Cai,Yanghao Wang,Ruibin Yuan,Yuanxing Zhang,Pengfei Wan,Wenhan Luo,Yike Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ReBind introduces a new framework to improve multi-reference image-conditioned video editing by embedding explicit reference tokens into editing instructions for better coordination of visual attributes and sources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video editing models struggle to accurately coordinate information from multiple visual sources due to unclear reference relationships and limitations in multimodal large language models (MLLMs).

Method: ReBind leverages semantic instructions embedded with reference tokens and includes two key developments: ReBind-Instruct, an MLLM trained to establish precise reference relationships, and ReBind-Edit, a system for adapting text-to-video models to coordinate visual attributes and sources.

Result: ReBind outperformed existing general-purpose MLLMs in instruction quality and achieved state-of-the-art results in multi-reference image-conditioned video editing among open-source methods.

Conclusion: Embedding explicit reference tokens addresses ambiguity in editing instructions and enhances video editing performance, marking a step forward in multi-reference video generation models.

Abstract: Recent diffusion-based video generation models have made significant progress in multi-reference image-conditioned video editing. However, existing methods still struggle to coordinate information from multiple visual sources accurately. We identify a critical deficiency in existing approaches. Existing editing instructions lack explicit reference relationships, and most multimodal large language models (MLLMs) cannot generate them reliably. To address this problem, we propose ReBind, a systematic framework that introduces semantic instructions with embedded reference tokens as the intermediate representation for multi-reference image-conditioned video editing. Our key insight is embedding reference tokens at semantic positions to eliminate ambiguity and establish precise bindings between visual attributes and their sources. We develop ReBind-Instruct, a specialized MLLM that learns to establish explicit bindings between visual attributes and their reference sources through a two-stage progressive scheme for precise reference relationships. We further develop ReBind-Edit, which enables lightweight adaptation of text-to-video models to coordinate multiple references by binding visual attributes to their designated sources. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ReBind substantially outperforms general-purpose MLLMs in instruction quality and achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source methods on reference image conditioned video editing. Our project webpage: https://rebind-mrv2v.github.io/.

</details>


### [186] [GlobalForge: Towards Robust AI-Generated Image Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14684)
*Manni Cui,Ruiqi Liu,Dianyuan Zou,Ziheng Qin,Jingrui Xu,ZiAn Wang,Jianglan Wei,Han Zhou,Yu Liu,Yan Wang,Shu Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper identifies the fragility in AI-generated image detectors and introduces a framework, GlobalForge, to shift from local to global structural cues, improving robustness against degradations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AIGI detectors overfit to local artifacts, making them fragile and ineffective under real-world degradations such as JPEG compression or blur. The motivation is to enhance the robustness of these detectors.

Method: The proposed method, GlobalForge, employs two modules: LIB to suppress local overfitting and GSR to enforce global evidence gathering. A contrastive structural loss ensures feature stability under degradations.

Result: GlobalForge achieves a significant improvement, with a 5.89% higher average balanced accuracy compared to previous state-of-the-art, and demonstrates strong performance on both single and compound degradations.

Conclusion: GlobalForge effectively shifts the focus from fragile local artifacts to robust global structural reasoning, improving detection performance across diverse real-world degradation scenarios.

Abstract: AI-generated image (AIGI) detectors achieve strong accuracy on clean benchmarks, but their performance drops sharply after images are propagated through real-world channels. We trace this fragility to what these detectors actually learn: they overfit to local artifacts left by generators in small spatial neighborhoods, which are easily destroyed by common propagation degradations such as JPEG compression and blur. Instead, we shift the discriminative cue from fragile local artifacts to more robust global structure. Building on this, we propose GlobalForge, a framework with two complementary modules. The Local Information Bottleneck (LIB) suppresses local components to block shortcut learning, while the Global Structural Reasoning (GSR) module forces every token to gather evidence from distant regions. Both modules are trained jointly under a contrastive structural loss based on degradation that keeps the resulting features stable under degradation. To support fine-grained robustness evaluation, we further introduce RealDeg-Bench, covering 7 common degradation operators and multi-step compound chains. GlobalForge improves average BAcc on 8 in-the-wild benchmark groups by $\mathbf{5.89\%}$ over the previous state-of-the-art, and is clearly ahead of representative baselines on RealDeg-Bench under both single and compound degradations. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/GlobalForge-BE0F/.

</details>


### [187] [Team RAS in 11th ABAW Competition: Multimodal Ambivalence Recognition Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14702)
*Elena Ryumina,Maxim Markitantov,Alexandr Axyonov,Fedor Shchetinin,Timur Abdulkadirov,Dmitry Ryumin,Alexey Karpov*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a computationally efficient multimodal model, Text Residual Fusion, for recognizing ambivalence and hesitancy in videos, achieving state-of-the-art results without using large model ensembles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addressing the challenges of recognizing ambivalence and hesitancy, which require processing inconsistent patterns across linguistic, acoustic, facial, and contextual modalities, while avoiding computationally expensive model ensembles.

Method: The authors propose a text-centered multimodal model called Text Residual Fusion, which uses text as the anchor modality and incorporates gated residual adjustments based on linguistic, acoustic, facial, and scene features.

Result: The model achieves a Macro F1-score of 75.14% on the Development and Public Test subsets and 78.24% on the Private Test subset, outperforming text-only models by 4.03%.

Conclusion: Text-centered multimodal fusion effectively integrates complementary information from multiple modalities, improving recognition performance for ambivalence and hesitancy, while being computationally efficient.

Abstract: Automatic recognition of ambivalence and hesitancy is challenging because these states may be expressed through inconsistent linguistic, acoustic, facial, and contextual patterns, while top-performing systems often rely on computationally expensive ensembles. We present a single text-centered multimodal approach for video-level ambivalence and hesitancy recognition for the 11th Affective & Behavior Analysis in-the-Wild (ABAW) Challenge. The proposed approach combines linguistic, acoustic, facial, and scene features using text-centered multimodal fusion model. Text Residual Fusion treats text as the anchor modality and applies gated residual adjustments based on the other modalities. Experiments on the Behavioural Ambivalence/Hesitancy (BAH) corpus confirm that text is the strongest unimodal modality. The Text Residual Fusion model achieves an average Macro F1-score (MF1) of 75.14% across the Development and Public Test subsets. On the Private Test subset, it reaches an MF1 of 78.24%, outperforming the text model by 4.03%. These results demonstrate that complementary multimodal information can improve recognition performance without requiring a large model ensemble.

</details>


### [188] [Pretraining Multiple Instance Learning Networks with Multi-Teacher Distillation from Pathology Slide Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14703)
*Mingxi Fu,Jiawen Li,Renao Yan,Jiali Hu,Qiehe Sun,Tian Guan,Yonghong He*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a distillation-based pretraining framework for multiple instance learning (MIL) in whole-slide image (WSI) analysis, leveraging slide-level foundation models as teachers to improve MIL's performance and transferability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MIL models face challenges like unstable optimization, overfitting, and limited transferability due to reliance on limited slide-level labels and the need for simultaneous learning of aggregation mechanisms and discriminative representations.

Method: The proposed approach uses a distillation-based pretraining framework that leverages two slide-level foundation models, TITAN and CARE, as teachers. An angular dispersion normalized distillation loss is introduced to balance supervision from these teachers, and the resulting weights are used to initialize MIL models for downstream tasks.

Result: Systematic evaluations on 15 benchmark datasets demonstrate that the pretraining approach improves MIL aggregator performance over training from scratch, especially in linear probing and few-shot settings. It also maintains computational efficiency.

Conclusion: Pretraining through knowledge distillation enhances the performance, stability, and transferability of MIL models in WSI analysis without increasing computational demands. This approach offers a robust alternative to training from scratch in computational pathology.

Abstract: Multiple instance learning (MIL) has become the main paradigm for whole-slide image (WSI) analysis in computational pathology. However, existing MIL aggregators are still typically trained from scratch for each downstream task, relying on limited slide-level labels to learn both aggregation mechanisms and downstream discriminative representations simultaneously. As a result, they often suffer from unstable optimization, overfitting, and limited transferability. Similar to pretrained ResNet and Vision Transformer models in natural image learning, MIL also requires reusable pretrained initialization. However, high-quality slide-level pretraining data remain scarce, and MIL models are usually lightweight and weakly supervised, making large-scale pretraining difficult in practice. To address this challenge, we propose a distillation-based pretraining framework for MIL, which leverages two slide-level foundation models, TITAN and CARE, as teachers to transfer their representational knowledge into a diverse set of MIL architectures. To effectively balance supervision from different teachers, we further introduce an angular dispersion normalized distillation loss. The distilled weights are then used as initialization for downstream adaptation. We conduct systematic evaluations on 15 benchmark datasets under both linear probing and full-parameter fine-tuning, and further validate its advantages in few-shot scenarios. Experimental results show that pretraining generally improves MIL aggregators over from scratch training, especially in linear-probing and few-shot settings, while maintaining the computational efficiency of lightweight MIL models. Code is available at https://github.com/fu0201/MIL_Pretrained.

</details>


### [189] [Variational Inference for Bird's Eye View Segmentation in Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14710)
*Jingyue Shi,Huaicheng Li,Junhui Zhao,Yanxiang Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel transformer-based method (TVB) for bird's-eye view (BEV) segmentation, which addresses challenges in fusing data from multiple cameras for autonomous driving.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite the importance of BEV in autonomous driving, challenges exist in effectively fusing data from multiple cameras, particularly in complex environments. The paper aims to address this gap.

Method: The authors propose a transformer-based variational flow transformation network (TVB) using a conditional variational autoencoder (CVAE) backbone to generate multiple BEV maps. They incorporate normalizing flows for expressive probability distributions and utilize a BEV-attention fusion (BAF) module for adaptive integration of BEV maps.

Result: Experimental evaluations using the nuScenes and OPV2V datasets show that the proposed TVB method outperforms others in multi-camera BEV segmentation and lane perception tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed TVB method is effective and superior in generating BEV representations by leveraging variational inference and transformer-based techniques, offering significant advancements in autonomous driving visual perception.

Abstract: The bird's eye view (BEV) has emerged as a pivotal approach for environmental perception in autonomous driving, providing a unified spatial representation for vehicles. Nevertheless, despite BEV's significance in addressing the challenges inherent to autonomous driving, effectively fusing data from multiple camera sensors and operating in complex external driving environments remains a considerable challenge. To mitigate this issue, we recast the BEV segmentation problem within a variational inference framework. In this paper, we propose a novel transformer-based variational flow transformation network for BEV segmentation, denoted as TVB. Our architecture implicitly learns the mapping from multiple camera views to a unified canonical BEV map during training by exploiting posterior BEV supervision. TVB employs a conditional variational auto encoder (CVAE) as its backbone and produces multiple BEV map candidates. To augment the realism of the generated BEV maps, we integrate normalizing flows into the map generation process, enabling the construction of more complex and expressive probability distributions. Furthermore, we design a BEV-attention fusion (BAF) module that harnesses attention mechanisms to adaptively integrate the multiple candidate BEV maps. Experimental results, evaluated on both the nuScenes and OPV2Vdatasets, demonstrate that our proposed method achieves superior performance in multi-camera view BEV segmentation and lane environment perception.

</details>


### [190] [VideoSEMA: a scalable and efficient Mamba-like attention for video understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14711)
*Nhat Thanh Tran,Fanghui Xue andShuai Zhang,Jiancheng Lyu,Yunling Zheng,Yingyong Qi,Jack Xin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces VideoSEMA, a video understanding model with a scalable split space-time attention mechanism, proving it is computationally efficient and equivalent to full space-time attention under certain conditions. Successful results are shown across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a scalable and computationally efficient attention model for video understanding tasks, overcoming the limitations of existing vision transformer and Mamba models.

Method: The method involves using a split space-time attention in VideoSEMA, which combines local window attention and global averaging for spatial processing in a Mamba-like architecture and a softmax temporal attention for temporal processing. The equivalence of this split approach to full space-time attention is mathematically proven under specific rank conditions.

Result: VideoSEMA outperforms heavier competing models (e.g., vision transformers, Mamba) on benchmark K400 and SSv2 datasets and maintains better accuracy under resolution scaling compared to VideoMamba.

Conclusion: VideoSEMA is a scalable and efficient video understanding solution, showing potential for analyzing longer videos with further development in its temporal attention mechanisms.

Abstract: We present for video understanding (classification) a split space-time attention model, VideoSEMA, consisting of a scalable and efficient Mamba-like attention (SEMA) block in space and a softmax temporal attention in time. In each frame, SEMA attention applies a local window attention in parallel with a global averaging in a Mamba macro-architecture, which is called Mamba-like. Under certain rank conditions, we prove that the computationally cheaper split space-time attention is equivalent to full space-time attention. On benchmark K400 data sets, VideoSEMA out-performs heavier vision transformer and Mamba models. On benchmark SSv2 data, VideoSEMA leads in top-1 accuracy among models of similar parameter sizes. As image resolution scales up from standard $224^2$ to $1024^2$ on K400 and without fine-tuning, VideoSEMA degrades much more gracefully than VideoMamba in accuracy. It is promising to extend VideoSEMA to longer videos with a dilated/sparse temporal attention.

</details>


### [191] [Causal-Adversarial Probing of Clinical Covariates for Prostate MRI Grading](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14720)
*Yipei Wang,Shiqi Huang,Wen Yan,Weixi Yi,Dean C. Barratt,Mark Emberton,Daniel C. Alexander,Veeru Kasivisvanathan,Yipeng Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a method to assess the influence of clinical covariates in MRI-based prostate cancer grading models using a causal reasoning adversarial framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the unexamined role of clinical covariates in prostate MRI-based cancer grading models, determining whether they encode useful or misleading shortcut information.

Method: Develops and applies an adversarial framework that suppresses the influence of clinical covariates, one at a time, while preserving MRI-based grade prediction, tested on a dataset of 3,479 MRI examinations.

Result: Suppressing clinical covariates like age, BMI, and alcohol use slightly improved prediction accuracy, while suppressing PSA and prostate volume significantly decreased accuracy, showing their relevance to the task.

Conclusion: Adversarial covariate suppression can differentiate between harmful and useful covariate dependence in MRI-based cancer grading, improving understanding and generalization of deep learning models.

Abstract: Deep learning models for prostate MRI-based cancer grading may encode clinical covariates that either reflect useful disease-related signal or non-generalising shortcut information, but their role is usually assumed. We propose a causal-reasoning framework for probing covariate dependence in MRI-based International Society of Urological Pathology (ISUP) Grade Group prediction. Rather than treating mpMRI as a direct cause of grade, we model MRI appearance and ISUP grade as observations of latent tumour pathology, and test whether candidate clinical variables act as nuisance correlates, disease-related proxies, or irrelevant covariates in the learned representation. We implement this using an adversarial framework that suppresses the decodability of individual clinical covariate at a time while preserving MRI-based grade prediction. The approach is developed and evaluated on 2,903 prostate MRI examinations, with external validation on 576 patients. We report a set of interesting and previously under-explored imaging-to-clinical-variable interactions in the context of deep learning generalisation. For examples, in binary ISUP Grade Group $\geq2$ classification, suppressing age, BMI, and alcohol use improved AUC by 1.23%, 0.84%, and 1.42%, respectively (all p < 0.05), suggesting reduced non-generalising covariate information; In contrast, suppressing PSA and prostate volume degraded AUC by 1.91% and 7.61% (all p < 0.001), indicating that these variables carried task-relevant signal. These findings show that adversarial covariate suppression can provide a practical representation-level analysis for distinguishing potentially harmful dependence from informative signal in prostate MRI grading models.

</details>


### [192] [Multimodality as Supervision: Self-Supervised Specialization to the Test Environment via Multimodality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14721)
*Kunal Pratap Singh,Ali Garjani,Rishubh Singh,Muhammad Uzair Khattak,Efe Tarhan,Jason Toskov,Andrei Atanov,Oğuzhan Fatih Kar,Amir Zamir*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a method called Test-Space Training (TST), which uses cross-modal learning and self-supervised pre-training on multimodal data collected in test-specific environments to achieve competitive performance without relying on large-scale internet datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from leveraging cross-modal learning as a self-supervised mechanism to develop strong representations from multimodal data found in fixed test environments, while reducing dependence on large-scale internet-based datasets.

Method: The method, Test-Space Training (TST), involves collecting multimodal data directly from a test environment and conducting self-supervised pre-training on it. The learned model is then evaluated on downstream tasks in the same environment, exploring a specialization-focused setup.

Result: The study finds that competitive performance can be achieved with TST when compared to generalist models like DINOv2 and CLIP, despite the latter being pre-trained on extensive internet-scale datasets. The approach highlights a reduced need for such external datasets.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that cross-modal learning and targeted pre-training in specific environments can substitute internet-scale datasets for tasks that require specialization. This approach not only reduces data requirements but also offers insights into trading off model generalization versus specialization through multimodal pre-training.

Abstract: Cross-modal learning, i.e., learning to predict one modality from another, is a fundamental mechanism for self-supervision via leveraging multimodality. Many practical applications, e.g., deploying a household robot, involve devices that are equipped with a rich set of sensors that enable multimodal sensing in their test environment. This presents an opportunity to apply cross-modal learning to the multimodal data sensed by these devices to learn representations. Findings in developmental psychology also suggest that biological agents leverage it to build an effective representation of their surroundings.
  To study this, we propose a controlled setup, where we restrict a user device to just a given test environment. It results in a specialization setup where we attempt to develop a performant model for this specific test environment. Under this setup, we develop Test-Space Training (TST), which performs multimodal data collection in the test environment and performs self-supervised pre-training on it. We evaluate these models on various downstream tasks in the same environment.
  Under this setup, we find various interesting insights, such as collecting rich multimodal data only from the test environment and leveraging cross-modal learning, we can achieve competitive results with generalist models (e.g., DINOv2 and CLIP) pre-trained on large-scale internet datasets. This enables an alternative scenario where the need for external Internet-scale datasets for pre-training models is reduced. We also present a set of analyses and ablations that raise intriguing points on substituting data with (multi)modality, and how varying pre-training data enables a tradeoff between a model's abilities to specialise to a test environment, and generalize to held-out spaces.

</details>


### [193] [AE-UAV: An Air-to-Air Event-Based UAV Tracking Benchmark and a Real-Time Frequency-Domain Tracker](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14726)
*Zixin Jiang,Bing He,Chaoran Xiong,Zhenzhen Wang,Xin Zhao,Ling Pei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces AE-UAV, the first air-to-air event-based dataset for UAV tracking, and proposes FSFT, a lightweight and efficient tracking method.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Developing real-time, resource-efficient airborne UAV tracking systems has been a challenge due to limited dynamic range and fixed temporal sampling in traditional cameras, coupled with the lack of event-based datasets and high computational requirements of existing models.

Method: The authors create AE-UAV, an airborne event-based dataset for UAV tracking, and propose FSFT, a training-free method combining frequency-domain template matching, search region prediction, and drift correction.

Result: FSFT achieves 93.97% accuracy compared to state-of-the-art GPU-based methods, runs at 420 FPS on a CPU, and shows significant efficiency and temporal resolution improvements.

Conclusion: AE-UAV and FSFT together offer a high-performance, resource-efficient solution for real-time air-to-air UAV tracking, addressing key gaps in existing methods.

Abstract: Air-to-air (A2A) unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) tracking is fundamental to airborne remote sensing of low-altitude aerial targets. However, the deployment of continuous, real-time tracking systems on UAVs presents significant challenges. In A2A scenarios, traditional frame-based cameras suffer from severe performance degradation under low illumination, overexposure, and high-speed motion owing to their limited dynamic range and fixed temporal sampling. Although event cameras offer a promising alternative with microsecond temporal resolution and a high dynamic range, current research is bottlenecked by two primary issues: 1) the absence of dedicated A2A event-based datasets, and 2) the heavy reliance of existing trackers on GPU acceleration and extensive training data, rendering them impractical for resource-constrained UAVs. To bridge these gaps, we introduce AE-UAV, an air-to-air event-based UAV tracking benchmark. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first airborne-captured event camera dataset for A2A tracking, comprising 178 flight sequences with continuous-time cubic B-spline annotations. Furthermore, we propose the Fast-Slow Frequency-domain Tracking (FSFT) method. This lightweight, training-free framework seamlessly integrates frequency-domain template matching with search region prediction and detection-based drift correction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FSFT operates at an ultra-fast 420 frames per second (FPS) on CPU-only hardware. It retains 93.97% of the accuracy of state-of-the-art GPU-dependent methods while delivering a 5.32-fold effective speedup and exhibiting superior temporal resolution generalization, thereby providing a highly efficient and robust solution for airborne remote sensing of aerial targets. The dataset and source code are available at https://github.com/MSP-xEN/AE-UAV.

</details>


### [194] [WorkDrive: Roadwork Chain of Causation for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14727)
*Tianyi Jiang,Wen Zhang,Sihan Yang,Ming Lu,Wentao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: WorkDrive improves autonomous driving VLMs in roadwork zones by introducing causal reasoning aligned with trajectory prediction, reducing trajectory error significantly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs for autonomous driving struggle to adapt to altered visual cues in roadwork zones, where conventional lane markings and permanent signs are replaced or absent.

Method: WorkDrive builds a framework incorporating perception-grounded causal reasoning using a multitask perception pipeline, enabling connection between scene elements and safe planning. Supervised and reinforcement learning techniques fine-tune predictions.

Result: Implementing WorkDrive's causal reasoning approach reduced trajectory error by 9.0% and a consistency-based reinforcement learning method achieved an additional 3.0% improvement on ROADWork dataset.

Conclusion: WorkDrive bridges the gap in visual reasoning for autonomous driving in work zone scenarios, showcasing significant improvements in trajectory prediction accuracy through structured causal reasoning.

Abstract: Autonomous driving vision-language models (VLMs) struggle in roadwork zones, where familiar visual cues such as lane markings and permanent signs are altered or absent, and temporary devices such as cones and barriers redefine the drivable corridor. VLMs can detect these objects, but without explicit guidance they anchor their reasoning on familiar elements from pre-training and fail to connect work-zone observations to correct planning decisions. We propose WorkDrive, a framework that constructs perception-grounded causal reasoning for work zones and aligns it with trajectory prediction. An automated multitask perception pipeline extracts structured scene facts and injects them into a Chain-of-Causation (CoC) annotation pipeline, redirecting the annotator's attention to domain-specific elements. The resulting reasoning labels are used for supervised fine-tuning, followed by reinforcement learning with a single reward: consistency between lateral meta-actions and the predicted trajectory. On ROADWork, the largest public work-zone dataset, the proposed roadwork CoC reduces trajectory average displacement error (ADE) by 9.0\%, and consistency-based GRPO yields a further 3.0\%, achieving progressive improvement over the trajectory-only baseline. Code and data will be publicly released.

</details>


### [195] [VQ-Touch: A Data-Efficient Tactile Generation Framework Across Sensors and Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14728)
*Kailin Lyu,Long Xiao,Jianing Zeng,Di Wu,Lin Shu,Jie Hao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VQ-Touch is a framework for tactile image generation, optimizing for diverse sensor and scenario applications through advanced data utilization and generalization methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current tactile image generation methods face challenges in efficiency, generalization, and handling vision-limited environments.

Method: The paper introduces DM-VQGAN for tactile representation and a discrete diffusion decoder for multimodal generation and enhanced generalization.

Result: Experiments demonstrate VQ-Touch outperforming existing state-of-the-art approaches across multiple tasks.

Conclusion: VQ-Touch is a versatile and robust solution for tactile image generation, addressing sensor dependency and broadening application capabilities.

Abstract: Tactile image generation significantly reduces the dependency on expensive and wear-prone sensors by synthesizing high-fidelity tactile data, offering an efficient solution for tactile information acquisition in robotic perception and human-machine interaction systems. However, existing methods depend on large-scale, diverse datasets from specific sensors and lack efficient data utilization and robust generalization capabilities, struggling in vision-limited environments. To address this, we introduce VQ-Touch, a tactile generation framework that supports both cross-sensor and multi-scenario applications. Specifically, to efficiently extract complex deformation and texture features from the data, we propose DM-VQGAN, an effective tactile representation learner. Furthermore, we introduce a discrete diffusion decoder with a unified conditioning interface, supporting multimodal generation tasks such as images and labels, and enhances the model's generalization capability through few-shot mixed training, thus achieving compatibility with current mainstream sensors and their variants. Experiments show that VQ-Touch surpasses state-of-the-art methods in multiple tasks.

</details>


### [196] [GeoDetect: Geometric Adversarial Detection for VLPs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14737)
*Afsaneh Hasanebrahimi,Hanxun Huang,Christopher Leckie,James Bailey,Sarah Erfani*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces GeoDetect, a method for detecting adversarial examples in vision-language pre-trained models (VLPs) using geometric properties of the embedding space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the vulnerability of vision-language pre-trained models (VLPs) to adversarial attacks, as existing detection methods primarily focus on single-modality models and overlook multimodal contexts.

Method: The authors conducted a theoretical analysis of VLP embedding spaces, identifying structured anisotropy, and demonstrated how adversarial examples shift representations out of manifold regions. GeoDetect employs geometric scores based on these properties to differentiate between clean and adversarial examples.

Result: GeoDetect effectively detects adversarial examples across varied VLP architectures, threat settings, and attack types (including adaptive attacks), showing robustness and reliability through comprehensive evaluations.

Conclusion: GeoDetect improves the safety and reliability of VLP models by leveraging off-manifold deviations in their embeddings as a basis for adversarial detection.

Abstract: Vision-language pre-trained models (VLPs) are widely used in real-world applications. However, they remain vulnerable to adversarial attacks. Although adversarial detection methods have demonstrated success in single-modality settings (either vision or language), their effectiveness and reliability in multimodal models such as VLPs remain largely unexplored. In this work, we study the geometry of VLP embedding spaces and observe structured anisotropy that differs from unimodal vision models. Our theoretical analysis shows that under this anisotropic structure, adversarial attacks increase the expected geometric separation between clean and adversarial examples (AEs). Specifically, we demonstrate that AEs consistently exhibit greater expected distances to randomly sampled points than their clean counterparts, indicating that AEs tend to push representations out of manifold regions. Building on these insights, we propose GeoDetect, which leverages these off-manifold deviations via geometric scores to identify AEs. Through comprehensive evaluations, we show that our approach reliably detects AEs across diverse VLP architectures and threat settings, covering unimodal and multimodal attacks as well as adaptive attacks, thereby providing a robust and practical approach to improving the safety and reliability of these models.

</details>


### [197] [FoMoVLA: Bridging Visual Foresight and Motion Guidance for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14739)
*Wei Li,Peijin Jia,Yuan Ma,Xuefeng Jiang,Titong Jiang,Sheng Sun,Yujian Li,Xin Wen,Han Hong,Zhikang Liu,Bailin Li,Kun Zhan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FoMoVLA enhances existing Vision-Language-Action models by introducing future feature foresight and sparse point tracking, aiming for improved visuomotor policy learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA models are reactive and lack explicit future prediction of world dynamics, while visual foresight methods don't provide motion guidance. The paper seeks to address these gaps.

Method: The framework combines future state prediction (goal state) with sparse point tracking (path to goal), using compact foresight tokens, point trajectories, and a cross-attention module for cohesive reasoning.

Result: FoMoVLA achieves state-of-the-art performance, demonstrating success across multiple datasets including LIBERO, RoboCasa GR-1 Tabletop, and LIBERO-Plus, with strong zero-shot generalization capabilities.

Conclusion: FoMoVLA's integration of foresight and motion tracking presents a significant advancement in visuomotor learning, offering enhanced prediction and motion guidance capabilities. 

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have achieved impressive results in visuomotor policy learning, yet remain fundamentally reactive, mapping current observations and language to actions without explicit forward prediction of world dynamics. Existing visual foresight methods predict future visual states but lack explicit motion guidance: they show where to go but not how to get there. We argue that future feature prediction and sparse point tracking are naturally complementary: the former provides the goal state, while the latter captures the continuous motion path toward it. We propose FoMoVLA, a framework that augments VLA representations with explicit spatio-temporal supervision by jointly learning future feature foresight and sparse 2D point tracking, enhancing the continuous action policy. FoMoVLA introduces compact foresight tokens to decode future feature states, decodes sparse temporal 2D point trajectories to model compact geometric motion, and couples both through a lightweight future-conditioned cross-attention module that enables consistent reasoning between anticipated states and point dynamics. Extensive experiments on LIBERO, RoboCasa GR-1 Tabletop, and LIBERO-Plus demonstrate state-of-the-art performance and strong zero-shot generalization. Project page is available at https://liauto-research.github.io/FoMoVLA.

</details>


### [198] [On the Disagreement in Perturbation-based xAI -- Benchmarking Perturbation Choices for Flood Detection from SAR Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14743)
*Anastasia Schlegel,Ronny Hänsch*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper investigates how parameter choices in perturbation-based explainable AI methods significantly affect the resulting relevance maps and interpretations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the sensitivity of perturbation-based xAI methods to parameter settings, particularly in flood detection using SAR imagery.

Method: Analyzing the effect of patch geometry and perturbation types on relevance estimation and evaluating consistency and faithfulness of relevance maps.

Result: Different perturbation settings lead to varied and sometimes contradictory explanations in relevance maps.

Conclusion: Careful evaluation and calibration of perturbation parameters are crucial for meaningful and robust xAI interpretations.

Abstract: Perturbation-based xAI methods are widely used to analyze the behavior and predictions of deep learning models. By altering input regions and measuring the resulting changes in class probabilities with respect to the original image, they assign relevance scores and generate heatmaps that reflect each region's contribution to the prediction.
