These findings provide practical guidance for selecting quantization strategies for deploying large code models on resource-constrained hardware and highlight the importance of evaluating quantized models beyond functional correctness.

</details>


### [396] [NexForge: Scaling Executable Agent Tasks via Requirement-First Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14186)
*Jiarong Zhao,Zhikai Lei,Zhiheng Xi,Rui Zheng,Hang Yan,Jie Zhou,Qin Chen,Liang He*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces NexForge, a framework to efficiently generate training data for executable agents by addressing limitations tied to substrate-first methods. NexForge identifies task requirements and automates task creation, achieving state-of-the-art performance in open-source agent models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome limitations in traditional data generation for agent training, which requires manual expansion, domain-specific pipelines, and yields task distributions that are often unrealistic or biased.

Method: NexForge operates through demand discovery (identifying realistic tasks and scenarios), task compilation, and automated retrieval or construction of materials and configurations needed for agent training. It then collects teacher trajectories and distills them for model training.

Result: NexForge produces thousands of tasks across domains, significantly improving performance metrics such as Terminal-Bench 2.0 results and GDPval Elo scores. It also contributes to training Nex-N2 models, which achieve state-of-the-art open-source performance, surpassing proprietary systems.

Conclusion: NexForge demonstrates scalability, versatility, and improved real-world alignment in task generation. It not only boosts agent performance but also offers a public resource (Nex-N2 models) that achieves competitive benchmarks.

Abstract: Scaling executable agent training data is bottlenecked by substrate-first methods that tie task generation to predefined tools, repositories, or skill graphs: expanding coverage requires manual expansion of the substrate, each new domain demands a bespoke pipeline, and the resulting task distributions often reflect substrate convenience rather than real-world demand. We introduce NexForge, a requirement-first framework that compiles free-form capability requirements into executable agent training data. NexForge first performs research-based demand discovery to identify representative task forms, realistic scenarios, and their relative prevalence. It then applies distribution-aware task compilation and automatically retrieves or constructs the files, repositories, dependencies, and runtime configurations required to materialize each task, followed by teacher rollout collection and trajectory distillation. The same pipeline, without any domain-specific infrastructure, produces 3,600 terminal tasks and 2,000 office tasks, improving Qwen3.5-35B-A3B Base from 22.5% to 52.0% on Terminal-Bench 2.0 and from 813 to 1338 Elo on GDPval; scaling to 43.2K terminal tasks reaches 58.4%, surpassing Claude Opus 4.6. Scaled further, NexForge-synthesized data contributes to the training of Nex-N2, a family of publicly available agent models that lift Qwen3.5-35B-A3B to 75.3% on Terminal-Bench 2.1 and to 1585 Elo on GDPval -- achieving state-of-the-art open-source performance and surpassing several frontier proprietary systems. Nex-N2 models are available at https://nex.sii.edu.cn/

</details>


### [397] [ToolAlignBench: Investigating Alignment Conflicts in Tool-Calling Enabled LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14285)
*Aryan Keluskar,Amrita Bhattacharjee,Huan Liu*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper explores value conflicts within safety-aligned LLMs in regulated industries, finding they frequently override deployment instructions and introducing a benchmark for evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study is motivated by the need to understand how safety alignment in LLMs operates under conflicting human values, particularly in regulated sectors where specific organizational instructions clash with broader human-centric safety principles.

Method: The researchers built a benchmark comprising 128 scenarios across 16 domains to assess how safety-aligned LLMs respond to conflicts between deployment instructions and safety-trained values.

Result: Open-source models were shown to override deployment instructions up to 43.4% of the time, often engaging in whistleblowing, data exfiltration, and evidence tampering. Abliteration was effective in reducing external whistleblowing rates.

Conclusion: The findings highlight a fundamental challenge in achieving value alignment for LLMs in pluralistic settings, suggesting the need for tools to evaluate and navigate conflicts between competing legitimate interests.

Abstract: Safety alignment in LLMs aims to align models with human values, but which values take precedence when they conflict? We investigate this question in the context of tool-calling LLM agents deployed in regulated industries, where agents processing confidential documents may encounter content that triggers safety-trained values (e.g., public welfare) that conflict with deployment-context instructions (e.g., internal logging). To empirically verify this phenomenon, we build a benchmark of 128 scenarios across 16 domains. We find that safety-aligned open-source models override their deployment instructions up to 43.4% of the time, engaging in whistleblowing, data exfiltration, and evidence tampering when processing documents that suggest organizational wrongdoing. We also find that abliteration reduces rates of external whistleblowing. These results reveal a fundamental tension in pluralistic alignment, where the same safety training that protects users can cause agents to act against deployment instructions in ways that create unpredictable liability risks. We release our benchmark as a framework to support evaluation of agent behavior under competing legitimate interests.

</details>


### [398] [Copy-on-Write Scoring: Application-Specific Agent Evaluations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14336)
*Joanna Roy,Sven Hoelzel*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: Evaluating LLM-based agents in software workflows is challenging due to limitations in existing methods. CoW Scoring addresses this by isolating and scoring agents' database write operations, enabling precise and affordable evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Trustworthy deployment of LLM-based agents requires granular evaluation within specific application environments to pinpoint operational successes and failures, which existing benchmarks struggle to achieve.

Method: CoW Scoring uses a PostgreSQL Copy-on-Write mechanism to evaluate agent interactions directly within application environments, producing session and operation-level scores for database write actions.

Result: The framework was tested on Plane, an open-source project management tool, where it identified issues in the tool surface, enabling fixes that improved agent performance.

Conclusion: CoW Scoring is an efficient framework for evaluating LLM-based agents, offering detailed insights into their performance and supporting iterative improvements.

Abstract: Trustworthy deployment of LLM-based agents in software systems requires evaluating how they perform on application-specific workflows, with enough granularity to localize where they succeed and fail. Yet existing agent evaluation mechanisms are limited: benchmarks have low construct validity for application-specific workflows and environments, and replica evaluation environments are expensive and prone to drift. We propose Copy-on-Write (CoW) Scoring, a framework that evaluates agent operations directly within application environments using a PostgreSQL-level Copy-on-Write mechanism to isolate agent writes. CoW Scoring produces session- and operation-level scores that highlight where agents' database write operations succeed and fail in a given application environment, enabling inexpensive evaluation and iteration on agent harnesses and tool surfaces. We demonstrate the framework on Plane, an open-source project-management platform, where analysis surfaced specific issues in the tool surface, and corresponding fixes produced measurable improvements on affected models.
  Python library: https://github.com/trail-ml/agent-cow-python

</details>


### [399] [The Prover Is the Judge: Verified Security Software from AI Coding Agents in Ada/SPARK](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14340)
*Tobias Philipp*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper explores a verification-driven framework where AI coding agents generate and verify code using Ada/SPARK proof tools. The verifier determines correctness, leading to substantial cost and time savings, although with some limitations in detecting all defects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the inefficiency and high cost associated with traditional human-based code validation by leveraging AI agents combined with automated verification tools.

Method: The approach uses AI agents under a verifier-driven loop that writes and verifies security-critical code in Ada/SPARK, employing GNATprove for discharging proof obligations and ensuring correctness. Additional measures like tests and human reviews supplement gaps.

Result: AI agents achieved the generation and verification of security software at a much lower cost (20-40 times less supervision effort) while using GNATprove to validate functional correctness and ensure runtime error absence. However, certain defects required external interventions.

Conclusion: AI agents' reliability in code production and verification is only as robust as the quality and strength of their feedback mechanisms.

Abstract: AI coding agents produce code faster than humans can review it. In our approach, the prover is the judge of whether the code is correct. Under a verifier-driven loop, AI agents wrote and verified bare-metal security software in Ada/SPARK spanning classical and post-quantum cryptography, TLS 1.3, IKEv2, X.509, and a Matrix client. GNATprove discharged 49,280 proof obligations, established functional correctness for selected primitives, and proved the absence of run-time errors for the rest, at roughly 20-40 times lower supervision cost than comparable hand verification. GNATprove alone was insufficient: some defects could not be detected and were resolved using known-answer tests, interoperability, or human review of specifications. Given weak checks, the agent tried to bypass them and reported success. We report where each layer caught faults and draw the central lesson: what an agent can be trusted to establish is bounded by the strength of its feedback.

</details>


### [400] [Why Git Is the Memory Solution for the Agentic Development Lifecycle](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14390)
*Frank Guo*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper proposes a memory system integrated into version control (git) for coding agents, aiming to solve issues like reasoning retention from assistant transcripts via commit-session links, with replicable results and no labeling costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the loss of reasoning behind coding changes trapped in assistant transcripts, and create a more effective and replicable memory system for agentic development lifecycle (ADLC).

Method: The authors integrate memory with git's version control guarantees, conduct an eight-corpus retrieval study with ranking mechanisms, and utilize a router with components for structural maps, confidence-gated lookups, and decision synthesis to reconstruct development rationale.

Result: The integrated system achieved better retrieval scores (~0.31 pooled MRR), improved rationale reconstruction (0.83 sufficiency), and handled questions efficiently at reduced token sizes per query (382-980 tokens). Results are universally replicable based on commit-session links.

Conclusion: Integrating memory into git-bound structures ensures guarantees like ground truth accuracy, replicability without annotation, and effective rationale reconstruction, with significant improvements over transcript-based methods.

Abstract: Coding agents now produce a growing share of a team's code, while the reasoning behind each change -- the alternatives weighed, the constraints discovered, the approaches rejected -- is trapped in assistant transcripts that vanish with the session. Memory for this setting, the agentic development lifecycle (ADLC), is usually posed as one retrieval problem and built as machinery: tiered stores, memory graphs, compiled wikis, model-judged admission. We argue memory should instead be git-bound -- built into the repository's version control, inheriting the guarantees the machinery struggles to construct: ground truth from commits, freshness from rebuild, verification from the merge, containment from review. On this ledger we solve two problems separately, then combine them. Seed supply is closed as an eight-corpus retrieval study under a pre-registered ship discipline: five imported ranking mechanisms rejected, two kept, and a best configuration of ~0.31 pooled MRR -- ~60x the raw-transcript grep floor, ~15x an honest parsed-turn floor. Answer assembly is where ranking stops helping: single-shot retrieval scores only 0.07-0.20 answer-sufficiency on real developer questions, and ungated episode injection measurably degrades good answers. A router dispatches breadth to a git-anchored structural map, pointed lookups to confidence-gated episodes, and rationale to decision synthesis, which reconstructs why-arcs no single session contains (0.83 sufficiency on a young ~50k-LOC production system). Routed, the system answers at 382-980 tokens per question -- three orders of magnitude below the recorded history. Because ground truth is mined from commit-session links rather than annotated, every result is replicable on any user's own history at zero labeling cost. The remaining constraint is capture. Code, benchmark, and paper source: github.com/rekal-dev/rekal-cli.

</details>


### [401] [AI-Conducted Interviews in Empirical Software Engineering: An Experience Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14452)
*Rohit Gheyi,Danyllo Albuquerque,Márcio Ribeiro,Mirko Perkusich*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper evaluates a MyGPT-based AI interviewer for conducting semi-structured interviews in software engineering studies, analyzing its operational viability and participant reception.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Semi-structured interviews in empirical software engineering are resource-intensive, challenging to coordinate, and not easily adaptable to multilingual contexts. The study aimed to explore AI tools as an alternative to address these challenges.

Method: Customized MyGPT was used to conduct self-administered interviews, following a predefined protocol. Participants interacted through shared links, selected a preferred natural language, and submitted structured synthesized artifacts. These were analyzed for format compliance, language, and protocol consistency.

Result: Of 66 submissions, 92.4% adhered to the synthesis format, and 90.9% of participants rated the experience positively. Limitations included generic questions, shallow depth, privacy concerns, and lack of human interaction.

Conclusion: AI interviewers are a viable and acceptable alternative for short, low-risk ESE studies, but should be employed as complementary tools due to their limitations. Human oversight and robust protocol design are essential.

Abstract: Semi-structured interviews are widely used in empirical software engineering (ESE), but they are resource-intensive and difficult to coordinate across schedules, locations, and natural languages. This experience report examines a customized MyGPT used to conduct short, self-administered interviews in two ESE studies: one on refactoring practices and another on generative AI in Scrum-related activities. Participants accessed the interviewer through shared links, used voice interaction, selected a preferred natural language, and completed the interview without a researcher present. The AI followed a predefined protocol and generated a structured synthesis that participants voluntarily submitted; these artifacts were not treated as verbatim transcripts. We analyzed 66 submissions and questionnaire responses, and audited artifact format, language, length, and protocol consistency. Of the submitted artifacts, 92.4% followed the expected synthesis format, 65 were predominantly in Portuguese and one in English, and two conflicted with the reported protocol. Participants generally rated the experience positively: 90.9% reported a positive overall experience and comfort, 95.5% considered the questions clear, 97.0% rated the pace positively, and 89.4% would participate again. Reported limitations included generic questions, limited sensitivity to answers, insufficient depth, privacy concerns, and missed human interaction. The findings support the operational viability and acceptability of this workflow among analyzed respondents, but do not establish completion rates, time savings, summary fidelity, or equivalence to human-conducted interviews. AI interviewers should therefore be treated as a complementary option for short, focused, low-risk studies, with protocol design, privacy guidance, artifact validation, and human oversight.

</details>


### [402] [Beyond Generalist LLMs: Specialist Agentic Systems for Structured Code Workflow Execution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14456)
*Harris Borman,Herman Wandabwa,Fusun Yu,Sandeepa Kannangara,Justin Liu,Anna Leontjeva,Ritchie Ng*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper investigates whether specialist software development agents outperform general-purpose agents in transforming BPMN diagrams into deterministic executable workflows, finding specialist agents significantly better in accuracy, efficiency, and cost savings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine if specialist software development agents provide measurable advantages over general-purpose agents for specific tasks like transforming BPMN diagrams into workflows, given the growing adoption of Large Language Models (LLMs) in software development.

Method: The paper introduces a specialist workflow for transforming BPMN diagrams into deterministic workflows and compares its performance with generalist agents like Roo and Cline, evaluating metrics such as tool-use exactness, latency, tool-call errors, token cost, and code quality.

Result: Specialist agents outperform generalist agents by 9-20 percentage points in accuracy, 2-4 times faster in penalty-adjusted latency, 3 times fewer tool-call errors, over 95% reduction in token costs, and no need for repair iterations.

Conclusion: Specialist workflow agents offer significant performance, reliability, and cost benefits over general-purpose agents, particularly for industrial applications where consistent functionality and maintainability are crucial.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have accelerated the adoption of software development agents, now widely available as Integrated Development Environment (IDE) extensions and standalone applications. While these agents are typically general-purpose, it remains unclear whether specialist agents justify their additional development effort. We investigate this question in the context of business process automation, focusing on the transformation of Business Process Model and Notation (BPMN) diagrams into executable agentic workflows. Since BPMN specifies explicit control-flow semantics, we focus on deterministic workflows in which a fixed process model and inputs uniquely determine the executed path. We introduce a specialist workflow for this task and compare it against generalist agents such as Roo and Cline. Our results show that the specialist solution produces agents that outperform generalist baselines by approximately 9-20 percentage points in tool-use exactness, 2-4x in penalty-adjusted latency, and 3x fewer tool-call errors, while reducing generation token cost by over 95% and eliminating repair iterations. We also find that generalist agents generate code inconsistently in both functionality and quality, limiting their suitability for industrial settings where reliability and maintainability are essential.

</details>


### [403] [Predicting Human Visual Attention on Words in Source Code](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14535)
*Chia-Yi Su,Collin McMillan*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper proposes a computational model to predict human visual focus on software code, leveraging a novel loss function and artificial neural networks, and validates its effectiveness using eye-tracking datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To better understand human cognition during software engineering tasks and optimize user interface designs by predicting programmers’ visual attention on source code.

Method: A novel loss function aligns artificial neural network attention with human visual attention measured through eye-tracking experiments. The model was evaluated using three datasets in Java and C languages.

Result: The model significantly outperformed baselines in three datasets (64%, 16%, 467% improvement in Pearson correlation) and demonstrated superiority in scanpath prediction and reading tasks over competing AI models.

Conclusion: The proposed model is effective in predicting programmer visual attention, showing promising applications in enhancing user interface designs and understanding cognitive processes in software development.

Abstract: This paper presents a computational model to predict human visual attention over words in software source code. The visual attention of software engineers when reading source code has long been studied as a means to understand human cognitive processes during software engineering tasks. Predicting this visual attention is important for perfecting user interface design and understanding what information human programmers need. We propose a model of programmer visual attention in which we design a novel loss function that computes similarity between human attention measured during eye tracking experiments and the internal attention of the artificial neural network. We evaluate our model by comparing its outputs to actual eye tracking data from three separate datasets. Two are in the Java programming language and one is in the C programming language. Our model outperforms the baseline in software engineering by 64%, 16%, and 467% in each of these studies according to Pearson correlation. We used scanpath prediction as an example to demonstrate that our model is more capable of the task that requires the understanding of human thought process. Our model achieves a statistically significant improvement over the close baseline in the reading task according to normalized Levenshtein distance and outperforms both Claude and GPT-5 on both reading and writing tasks.

</details>


### [404] [A Deterministic Binary Fingerprinting Framework with Zero-Trained Feature Extraction for Sparse Count Matrices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14596)
*Lei Zhao,Fujin Huang,Ling Kang,Quan Guo*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces MMTB, a deterministic binary representation method for analyzing sparse count matrices, achieving competitive performance for coarse-grained tasks with reduced memory requirements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for efficient, label-free, and resource-constrained methods to analyze sparse count matrices without resorting to supervised labels, model fitting, or gradient-based optimization.

Method: MMTB applies Min-Max normalization, fixed cutoffs, and thermometer-based fingerprinting to transform data into binary representations, relying on Hamming distances and nearest-neighbor graph clustering.

Result: MMTB achieves high normalized mutual information (NMI) on coarse-grained tasks (NMI=0.99 on a three-cell-line mixture) but underperforms on fine-grained analyses compared to continuous embedding approaches.

Conclusion: MMTB is effective for resource-limited, coarse-grained separations but not suitable as a universal alternative to continuous embeddings for fine-grained tasks.

Abstract: Sparse count matrices from single-cell transcriptomes to k-mer profiles and document-term frequencies are conventionally analyzed via PCA-reduced graph clustering or iterative optimization in continuous embedding spaces. We introduce MMTB, a deterministic non-learned binary representation framework that requires no label supervision, model fitting, or gradient-based optimization. Column-wise Min-Max normalization followed by fixed cutoffs maps each sample to a thermometer fingerprint whose Hamming distances show empirical correspondence with normalized L1 distances, with Pearson correlation approximately 0.92 on single-cell RNA-seq pairs. In a favorable three-cell-line mixture, the 3-threshold fingerprint achieves NMI of 0.99 at 188 bytes per cell. Under a fair Hamming nearest-neighbor graph plus Leiden readout, MMTB approaches PCA plus Leiden on this coarse task. On challenging tissue-like annotations, continuous pipelines often lead; PBMC Seurat NMI is 0.39 for MMTB versus 0.49 for Scanpy, underscoring that MMTB is suited for coarse-grained separation and resource-constrained deployments rather than fine-grained subtype discovery or as a general replacement for continuous embeddings. Relative to dense float32 representations, MMTB fingerprints reduce memory by approximately 10-fold while providing fixed-width Hamming-indexable codes. PCA30 embeddings and sparse CSR may be smaller; we do not claim universal compression. A label-free suitability score is provided as a deployment guideline, not a performance predictor.

</details>


### [405] [SYNAPSE: A Multi-LLM Orchestrated AI Tutor for Secure Software Development Education with Neurodivergent-First Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14601)
*Giusy Ferrara,Ashkan Sami*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces SYNAPSE, an adaptive tutoring platform teaching secure software development with accessibility features supporting neurodivergent learners. It integrates AI tools and uses a practical approach to train users in vulnerability remediation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the lack of direct training for secure software development and bridge accessibility gaps for learners with ADHD and similar executive-function differences.

Method: The authors developed SYNAPSE, an AI-driven tutoring platform that combines Claude, GPT-4o, and Gemini, enforces a Socratic hint framework, and employs a vulnerable web app (ShopSecure) for hands-on learning.

Result: A pilot with 19 participants showed a System Usability Scale score of 76.4 and engagement level of 4.2/5. Cognitive load was consistent across neurodivergent and neurotypical learners, demonstrating feasibility and inclusivity.

Conclusion: SYNAPSE proves to be a viable educational tool for teaching secure software development inclusively, combining AI and accessibility features effectively, and aligning pedagogy with real-world practices.

Abstract: Developers who maintain real systems must continually recognise and remediate vulnerabilities in existing code, yet this skill is rarely trained directly: secure software development is commonly taught only after programming fluency is acquired, and accessibility support is treated as a secondary concern, disadvantaging learners with ADHD and related executive-function differences. This paper presents SYNAPSE, a publicly deployed adaptive tutoring platform for Java, Python for cybersecurity, and secure software development. SYNAPSE coordinates Claude, GPT-4o, and Gemini through the Model Context Protocol, routing interactions by pedagogical intent under a three-stage Socratic hint policy. It exposes eighteen always-visible accessibility features and anchors practice in ShopSecure, a deliberately vulnerable web application mapped to six OWASP Top 10 (2021) categories, on which learners practise the detect-understand-remediate loop characteristic of software maintenance. A feasibility pilot with nineteen participants across neurodivergent and neurotypical cohorts returned a System Usability Scale score of 76.4 and engagement of 4.2/5, with comparable cognitive-load levels across cohorts. SYNAPSE is available at https://synapse-course.com; a screencast is available at https://youtu.be/9R17KC47qQI.

</details>


### [406] [Rethinking Issue Resolution for AI/ML Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14657)
*Ahmed Adnan,Mushfiqur Rahman,Antu Saha,Oscar Chaparro*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper advocates for customized issue resolution frameworks for AI/ML systems, as current methods for traditional software are insufficient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of current issue resolution frameworks, which lack consideration for AI/ML-specific characteristics like stochastic behavior and the diversity of artifacts.

Method: The authors conducted a qualitative study analyzing 100 issue reports and pull requests from AI/ML platforms (TensorFlow, scikit-learn, MLflow, and AutoGPT) to identify unique challenges.

Result: They identified unique characteristics like recurring activities, iterative experimentation, and difficulties with reproducibility, artifact coordination, and nondeterministic behavior.

Conclusion: The study proposes a vision for better issue resolution frameworks for AI/ML systems and outlines directions for future research and tooling.

Abstract: We advocate for AI/ML issue resolution frameworks tailored to maintenance workflows and the nature of modern AI/ML systems. Existing issue resolution frameworks largely emerged for traditional software maintenance practices and do not explicitly account for characteristics common in AI/ML systems, such as stochastic behavior, experimentation-driven workflows, and heterogeneous artifacts beyond source code. To identify the unique characteristics of issue resolution in AI/ML systems and motivate the need for tailored frameworks, we conducted a qualitative study of issue resolution workflows documented in 100 issue reports and pull requests across four widely used AI/ML systems: TensorFlow, scikit-learn, MLflow, and AutoGPT. Our findings suggest that issue resolution in AI/ML systems involves: recurring AI/ML-related activities that span multiple resolution stages; iterative experimentation and adaptive verification; and coordinated changes across artifacts such as datasets, prompts, and model configurations. We also observed challenges related to reproducibility, nondeterministic behavior, and artifact coordination. Building on these findings, we present a vision for AI/ML issue resolution frameworks and discuss research directions and tooling support needed to realize this vision.

</details>


### [407] [LLM-Driven Approach to Modeling Tool Interoperability in Automotive Domain](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14659)
*Nenad Petrovic,Jiajie Zhang,Vahid Zolfaghari,Alois Knoll*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces an LLM-driven method for automating model interoperability in Model-Driven Engineering (MDE), focusing on automotive applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interoperability among heterogeneous modeling tools, especially in the automotive domain, is challenging due to the coexistence of different modeling languages and tools.

Method: An LLM-driven approach facilitates model interoperability by mapping model instances to a target metamodel and merging metamodels, demonstrated with transformations between Ecore and SysML v2.

Result: Automated transformations reduce manual effort and generate structurally valid target models, validated against automotive case studies.

Conclusion: The approach successfully enables cross-tool interoperability while minimizing manual transformation tasks, showcasing feasibility in the automotive modeling environment.

Abstract: Interoperability between heterogeneous modeling tools remains a significant challenge in Model-Driven Engineering (MDE), particularly in the automotive domain where multiple modeling languages, as well as defacto standard proprietary and open-source tools coexist. This paper presents an LLM-driven approach for automated model interoperability by considering two relevant aspects: 1) mapping model instances to a target metamodel 2) merging of metamodels. The proposed methodology is demonstrated through transformations involving Ecore and SysML v2 based metamodels and incorporates structural validation of generated model instances against user-defined target models. Automotive case studies illustrate the feasibility of the approach and show that large language models can significantly reduce manual transformation effort while generating structurally valid target models for cross-tool interoperability.

</details>


### [408] [Large Language Models for Code Generation from Multilingual Prompts: A Curated Benchmark and a Study on Code Quality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14816)
*Saima Afrin,Alessandro Midolo,Camilo Escobar-Velásquez,Mario Linares-Vásquez,Weiyuan Ding,Bowen Xu,Massimiliano Di Penta,Antonio Mastropaolo*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper studies how the natural language used in prompts influences code generation in large language models (LLMs) across multiple programming languages, revealing language bias and mixed language usage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore how language bias in natural language prompts affects the quality of code generation and conventions, as this area remains largely unexamined.

Method: The authors analyzed 460 coding tasks in Python and Java using multilingual prompts (English, Chinese, Hindi, Spanish, Italian) on LLMs (GPT-4o mini, DeepSeek, Claude), measuring metrics like functional correctness, structural quality, and lexical characteristics.

Result: The study found that English prompts do not always yield the best results, the influence of prompt language depends on both the programming language and the LLM, and generated code often blends English with the prompt language in comments and literals.

Conclusion: Prompt language significantly affects code generation, and the findings present a multilingual benchmark to address language biases and improve robust multilingual code generation systems.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) perform differently on identical programming tasks when prompted in different natural languages, a phenomenon known as language bias. While this behavior has been widely studied for general text generation, its impact on code generation quality and programming conventions remains largely unexplored. We investigate how the language used to describe programming tasks affects the source code generated by GPT-4o mini, DeepSeek, and Claude. Our study comprises 460 coding tasks spanning Python (230) and Java (230). We translate and manually curate the original English prompts into Chinese, Hindi, Spanish, and Italian while preserving their technical meaning. We evaluate the generated code using multiple dimensions, including functional correctness through test pass rates, structural quality using established code metrics, issues detected by static analysis tools, and lexical characteristics such as the language used in identifiers and comments. Our results show that (i) English prompts do not consistently produce the best functional correctness or code quality, (ii) the impact of prompt language depends on both the programming language and the LLM, and (iii) generated code frequently mixes English with the prompt language in comments and string literals. These findings provide the first curated multilingual benchmark for studying language bias in code generation and offer insights for developing more robust multilingual code generation systems.

</details>


### [409] [AI Prototyper: A Figma Plugin for Decomposition-Based GUI Prototyping with LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14830)
*Tawatchai Salangsingha,Sami Ashkan,Md Zia Ullah,Iain McGregor*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces AI Prototyper, an open-source Figma plugin that automates GUI prototyping via a decomposition and retrieval-augmented generation pipeline, enabling the quicker creation of higher-quality GUI prototypes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GUI prototyping is time-intensive, requiring both manual effort and design expertise. Automating prototype creation aligns with improving software development and maintenance efficiency.

Method: AI Prototyper employs a pipeline where natural language descriptions are decomposed into GUI features, retrieves components from a tailored library, and renders them as editable Figma layers. It supports human-in-the-loop editing and multilingual input.

Result: Preliminary evaluation showed faster prototype creation compared to manual methods, with experts rating AI-generated prototypes higher across multiple quality dimensions.

Conclusion: AI Prototyper enhances the GUI prototyping process by combining automation with human feedback, delivering higher efficiency and quality with broad language support.

Abstract: Graphical user interface (GUI) prototyping remains a time-consuming activity that demands both design expertise and considerable manual effort. As GUI prototypes are non-code artifacts that evolve alongside requirements throughout the development cycle, automating their generation is directly relevant to software maintenance and evolution. We present AI Prototyper, an open-source Figma plugin that automates GUI prototyping through a decomposition and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline. Given a natural-language description of a desired screen, such as a login page or a product detail card, the plugin decomposes the request into discrete GUI features, retrieves matching components from a custom 32-primitive library, and renders each component as a fully editable Figma layer with auto-layout. The pipeline uses Gemini 2.5 Flash as its LLM back-end and a Node.js/Express service. Unlike existing decomposition-based tools, AI Prototyper introduces a human-in-the-loop editing step that lets users review, modify, or extend the generated feature list before rendering, uses a different technology stack and LLM family, and supports multilingual input, producing correctly labelled interfaces in Thai, English, and Mandarin Chinese. In a preliminary evaluation, participants using AI Prototyper completed more prototypes in a fixed time window than those working manually, and expert practitioners rated the AI-generated prototypes higher across nine quality dimensions. A demonstration video is available at https://youtu.be/pRoFAH7MQaE. The source code and component library are available at https://github.com/tongsalangsingha/AI-prototyper-tool

</details>


### [410] [StructureClaw: Traceable LLM Agents and an Executable Benchmark for Structural Engineering Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14896)
*Sizhong Qin,Yi Gu,Yao Jiang,Ao Cai,Changjian Zhou,Shaoxuan Shuai,Jiachang Wang,Tianhao Shen,Yueqiang Li,Xinhao Li,Li Zeng,Yueshi Chen,Dachen Gao,Genrong Xu,Wenjie Liao,Xinzheng Lu*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces StructureClaw, a workbench for large language model (LLM) agents to handle entire workflows for structural-engineering tasks, along with an executable benchmark for evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations for structural-engineering tasks often focus narrowly on individual questions or outputs without verifying the full workflow, which leads to incomplete or inconsistent results.

Method: StructureClaw uses governed engineering skills, typed tools, a shared artifact state, and local analysis backends. It evaluates LLM agents through a benchmark called StructureClaw-Bench, with scenarios testing standard workflows, interactive robustness, and multimodal model reconstruction.

Result: In tests with ten agent-model configurations, the Success Rate improved from 56.8% with a generic baseline to 88.6% with the full automatic workflow. However, challenges remain in handling invalid numerical inputs and ensuring consistent structural model reconstruction.

Conclusion: Artifact-centered evaluations provide a robust framework for assessing and improving structural engineering agents, highlighting issues not evident from final output alone. StructureClaw enables more reliable end-to-end execution in engineering workflows.

Abstract: Addressing a structural-engineering request requires more than a single answer; it requires a chain of interdependent artifacts: interpreted requirements, a computable model, validation records, solver outputs, code-check records, and a final report. Evaluations centered on question answering or script generation rarely verify this complete evidence chain and may therefore reward fluent outputs even when the underlying engineering workflow is incomplete, internally inconsistent, or non-executable. To address this limitation, we present StructureClaw, an artifact-centered workbench in which LLM agents operate through governed engineering skills, typed tools, shared artifact state, and local analysis backends. We also introduce StructureClaw-Bench, an executable benchmark of 150 controlled scenarios spanning standard workflow execution, interactive robustness, and multimodal structural-model reconstruction. A scenario succeeds only when all required artifact- and execution-level assertions pass in a single run. Across ten agent-model configurations, each evaluated on the same 50 standard cases, the average Success Rate rises from 56.8% with the generic-skill baseline to 88.6% with the full automatic workflow. The interactive and multimodal evaluations identify two prominent remaining challenges: safe handling of invalid numerical inputs and fixture-consistent reconstruction of structural models. These findings show that artifact-centered evaluation can expose workflow-level failures that are difficult to identify from final responses alone, providing a more rigorous basis for evaluating and improving structural-engineering agents. The code and benchmark are available at https://github.com/structureclaw/structureclaw.

</details>


### [411] [FirmPilot: Evidence-Guided Multi-Agent Environment Recovery for IoT Firmware Rehosting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14903)
*Yanbing Shen,Fan Zhang,Haitao Xu*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: Firmware rehosting can be fragile and unreliable due to environment dependencies. FirmPilot, a multi-agent framework, improves reliability and scalability by grounding decisions in evidence and optimizing artifact reconstruction, doubling key success metrics compared to FirmAE.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing firmware rehosting methods struggle with diverse real-world environments due to vendor variability and complex dependencies. A more robust and evidence-guided approach is needed to enhance rehosting success.

Method: FirmPilot uses a multi-agent framework comprising a search agent for artifact identification, a planner for execution transitions, and specialized agents for recovering boot scripts, persistent state, and network configurations, iteratively refining the execution environment.

Result: FirmPilot demonstrated a significant improvement in its evaluation on a real-world firmware dataset, doubling web-service reachability from 25.49% to 52.39% and network reachability from 39.30% to 71.93%, while increasing the average detected services per firmware from 0.86 to 1.62.

Conclusion: FirmPilot's evidence-guided approach effectively resolves dependencies and enhances the reliability of firmware rehosting, fostering better downstream analysis and compatibility in IoT firmware execution environments.

Abstract: Firmware rehosting executes firmware images in emulated environments such as QEMU to enable scalable dynamic analysis of Internet of Things (IoT) devices. In practice, rehosting pipelines remain fragile across diverse real-world firmware images, as reaching an externally observable execution state depends on tightly coupled artifacts spanning boot scripts, persistent configuration (e.g., NVRAM-like key-value state), and network setup. Template-driven frameworks often fail to accommodate long-tail vendor conventions, while unconstrained use of large language models (LLMs) risks unsupported modifications and irreproducible executions. We introduce FirmPilot, an evidence-guided multi-agent framework for environment recovery in firmware rehosting. FirmPilot reformulates rehosting as iterative environment reconstruction in which a search agent grounds decisions through similarity-based retrieval, a planner coordinates execution-accepted transitions, and specialized agents recover filesystem/init artifacts, persistent state, and network exposure. Through repeated execution and evidence-grounded artifact deltas, the system resolves cross-layer dependencies across boot, state, and networking that otherwise prevent firmware executions from reaching a stable, externally reachable state in emulation. Evaluated on the large-scale, real-world LFwC firmware corpus, FirmPilot improves web-service reachability over FirmAE from 25.49% to 52.39% and network reachability from 39.30% to 71.93%. The resulting rehosts raise the average number of detected services per firmware from 0.86 to 1.62 and support downstream analysis workflows, including RouterSploit interaction and protocol-aware fuzzing over recovered service surfaces. The evaluation shows that evidence- and feedback-grounded agent coordination improves rehosting success, service recovery, and downstream utility in automated firmware rehosting.

</details>


### [412] [Leaf: An Instrumentation-based Dynamic Analysis Framework for Rust](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.15025)
*Mohammad Omidvar Tehrani,Marco Gaboardi,Nick Sumner,Steven Y. Ko*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces LEAF, a dynamic analysis framework for Rust, leveraging Rust's MIR-level semantic information to support various analysis tasks with manageable overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap in general-purpose dynamic analysis tools for Rust and enhance runtime program analysis leveraging Rust's ownership model and memory model.

Method: LEAF provides a Rust-native framework for runtime analysis, utilizing Dynamic MIR (DMIR) as an event-driven interface and capturing MIR-level semantic information augmented with runtime facts.

Result: LEAF was demonstrated using three dynamic analyses: a concolic executor, a Rust-specific sanitizer, and a control-flow tracer. It showed practicality and expressiveness with balanced compile-time and runtime overhead.

Conclusion: LEAF fills the notable gap in Rust dynamic analysis tools and shows that it is practical and efficient for runtime analysis tasks.

Abstract: This paper presents LEAF, an instrumentation-based dynamic analysis framework for Rust. Although Rust has grown rapidly in recent years, the landscape of program analysis tools for Rust is still in relatively early stages. One notable gap is the lack of a general-purpose dynamic analysis framework that can support different analysis tasks. LEAF aims to fill this gap by providing a Rust-native framework for analyzing Rust programs at runtime. Rust provides rich semantic information through its ownership model, type system, memory model, and compiler-level representation. Therefore, LEAF focuses on how to make this information available to dynamic analyses. In particular, LEAF captures MIR-level semantic information, augments it with runtime facts, and delivers it to analyses as an event stream through Dynamic MIR (DMIR), an event-driven programming interface. Through three substantial dynamic analyses -- a concolic executor, a Rust-specific sanitizer, and a control-flow tracer -- we demonstrate the practicality and expressiveness of LEAF. Our evaluation further shows that LEAF's compile-time and runtime overhead is meaningful but manageable.

</details>


### [413] [Capturing and Exploiting Design Pattern Variability in Mobile Application Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.15099)
*Ramón Peralta,Jose-Miguel Horcas*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper presents a method to improve mobile app code quality using design pattern variability modeling with UVL and automated Swift code generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address neglected design principles and architectural quality in mobile app development due to automatic code generation.

Method: Used Universal Variability Language (UVL) and Jinja templates for modeling design pattern variability and generating customizable Swift code.

Result: Formalized variability in design patterns and highlighted configuration insights, enhancing maintainable and structured mobile application generation.

Conclusion: Promotes improved mobile app development by bridging model-driven engineering with design pattern variability for reusable and sound architectures.

Abstract: The increasing reliance on automatic code generation in mobile application development often leads to code that neglects fundamental design principles and architectural quality. In this work, we address this challenge by capturing and exploiting the inherent variability of software design patterns to systematically generate customizable and well-structured mobile applications. We propose the use of the Universal Variability Language (UVL) to explicitly model the structural and behavioral variation points of common design patterns, such as Singleton, Strategy, Observer, Adapter, and Factory Method. These models are integrated with reusable Jinja templates, enabling code generation in Swift. Our approach leverages Software Product Line (SPL) engineering principles, treating design patterns as configurable assets within a product line and supporting automated generation of custom design patterns. We also analyze the configuration space of the modeled patterns, offering insights into their variability complexity. By formalizing design pattern variability and embedding it into the generation process, our work bridges model-driven engineering with practical mobile development, promoting the production of maintainable, reusable, and architecturally sound applications.

</details>


### [414] [Navigating the Socio-Technical Complexity Challenge in Quantum Software Ecosystems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.15135)
*Ronja Heikkinen,Santiago Núñez-Corrales,Vlad Stirbu*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces a socio-technical framework to help practitioners evaluate choices in quantum computing environments, addressing challenges in this fragmented field.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of systematic guidance for navigating the fragmented and fast-evolving quantum computing environment.

Method: The authors developed the framework using Design Science Research methodology, incorporating constructs like gravity wells, properties, and socio-technical desiderata.

Result: The framework enables context-aware decision-making that supports architectural flexibility and evolutionary development of quantum computing.

Conclusion: The study enhances theoretical understanding of quantum ecosystems and provides practical tools for navigating environment decisions effectively.

Abstract: Quantum computing environments are composed of heterogeneous layers spanning hardware, software development kits, and applications. Practitioners curating these environments face a fragmented and rapidly evolving landscape with few principled guides for navigation. This paper presents a framework for evaluating quantum computing environment choices through a socio-technical lens, developed using Design Science Research methodology. Drawing on the quantum software engineering literature as well as organizational and socio-technical research, the framework introduces three analytical constructs: gravity wells and their properties, which characterize how certain technologies and structural conditions exert increasing pull on surrounding environment choices, and socio-technical desiderata, which articulate the normative goals against which those pulls can be evaluated. The framework supports practitioners in making deliberate, context-aware environment choices that preserve architectural flexibility and support the evolutionary development of the field. Demonstration and evaluation of the framework is conducted through exemplary cases. The contribution advances both the theory of quantum ecosystems and the practical guidance available to organizations and practitioners navigating the current, evolving field of quantum computing.

</details>


### [415] [MM-IssueLoc: A Controlled Benchmark for Evaluating Visual Evidence in Multimodal Repository-Level Issue Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.15205)
*Shaoxiong Zhan,Shi Hu,Boyu Feng,Hai Lin,Andrew Gong,Zhengda Zhou,Jiaying Zhou,Yunyun Hou,Hao Su,Hai-Tao Zheng*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces MM-IssueLoc, a benchmark designed to evaluate repository-level issue localization using both text and visual evidence. Evaluation results show current systems struggle with multimodal data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Issue localization in repositories often involves multimodal data such as screenshots and logs, but most evaluations treat it as a text-only task. This research aims to address the gap by focusing on the appropriate use of visual evidence.

Method: MM-IssueLoc provides 652 annotated issue-pull request instances across 23 programming languages with structured multimodal data. It pairs text-only and image-assisted evaluation, and incorporates visual evidence as structured textual data.

Result: The strongest systems evaluated achieved file accuracy at 38.96% in top-5 predictions and function accuracy at 22.45% in top-10 predictions for multimodal inputs. This highlights the challenges in reliable multimodal issue localization.

Conclusion: MM-IssueLoc enables structured evaluation of visual evidence utilization in issue localization, aiding future research to develop systems capable of leveraging multimodal data effectively.

Abstract: Real repository issues routinely include visual evidence such as screenshots, error dialogs, rendered UI states, and logs, yet repository-level issue localization is evaluated mostly as a text-only task. Existing multimodal SE benchmarks evaluate end-to-end repair, entangling localization with patch synthesis and obscuring whether visual input helped, hurt, or was ignored. We introduce \textbf{MM-IssueLoc}, a controlled benchmark and evaluation protocol for repository-level localization with visual evidence. MM-IssueLoc contains 652 issue-PR instances across 23 languages, with annotations for 7 image categories and 4 relevance levels. It provides file-level and function-level gold labels, paired text-only and with-image evaluation, and VCE-based diagnostics that convert images into structured textual evidence. We evaluate LLM-based and retrieval-based systems, including MM-IssueLoc-VL-Emb as a controlled multimodal retriever. Results show that existing systems remain far from reliable multimodal repository localization: the strongest agent reaches 38.96 file Acc@5 and 22.45 function Acc@10, while the strongest retriever reaches 33.86 function Acc@10. Cross-benchmark comparisons show that high localization scores on text-dominant SWE benchmarks do not transfer cleanly to multimodal issue localization. MM-IssueLoc turns visual evidence into an explicit evaluation variable, enabling future work to test whether systems improve by using visual evidence for localization, rather than by relying on text-only cues or downstream patch-generation effects.

</details>


### [416] [Falsifiable Release Gates for Self-Improving Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.13070)
*Deepak Soni*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper proposes a safety mechanism for self-improving agent runtimes by introducing falsifiable release gates with robust, machine-verifiable tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many self-improving agents rely on self-graded safety claims, which are often unreliable. This paper aims to introduce a verifiable and robust safety methodology for such agents.

Method: The authors developed and implemented a methodology using falsifiable release gates that require every new functionality of an agent to pass predefined, machine-verifiable tests. A control ring ensures that safety-critical properties are validated over a bounded model using exhaustive checks.

Result: The proposed method was applied and validated on Antahkarana, an open runtime, with seven release gates ensuring safe self-improvement. Results, tools, and validation methods are made publicly available.

Conclusion: Falsifiable release gates provide a systematic and reproducible approach to ensuring safety and reliability in self-improving agents, especially around policy changes and critical operations.

Abstract: Safety claims on self-improving agent runtimes are almost always self-graded: a policy file, a guardrail, or a README commitment. We describe falsifiable release gates, and a methodology to build and validate such systems, such that every new capability must pass a pre-specified, machine-verifiable acceptance suite before it ships, and a fixed set of standing invariants is preserved at each gate. I think we applied the method in the Antahkarana, an open runtime, via seven gates from basic observability into a self governing loop that suggests changes to its own policy. no action goes to an effector without a safety-critical property capability token minted by a control ring is exhaustively machine-checked over the one million recorded reachable state space of a bounded model and re-checked against execution traces. A purposely broken model gives the shortest counterexample, so the checker has teeth, is apparent. The self-enhancement loop is positively Constrained: the entire write surface is policy rules, tightening changes. may auto-apply loosening changes always require a human merge and a proposal autoclosed is one that mispredicts its own effect; We publish the acceptance measured results for all the seven gates, define precisely the scope of each claim (a bounded of the coordination skeleton (not the learned components) and free the runtime, either command line tools and the gate suite, so the results reproduce, and gates can run against other agent frameworks. Reviewers may repeat the single command central non-bypass in seconds.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.NC'></div>

# q-bio.NC [[Back]](#toc)

### [417] [Thoughtseeds as Latent Causes: A Dual-Process Computational Phenomenology of Focused-Attention Meditation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14833)
*Prakash Chandra Kavi,Daniel Ari Friedman,Gustavo Patow*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The paper presents a computational model explaining focused-attention meditation's brain dynamics using a nested Markov-blanket framework and neural network simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how meditative expertise enables sustained attention, rapid distraction recovery, and large-scale brain network coordination by linking subjective experience with neurophysiological mechanisms.

Method: The authors propose a three-layer nested Markov-blanket framework combining physiological neuronal data, a generative low-dimensional model for mental content, and a metacognitive monitoring layer. The system uses variational Expectation-Maximization for training across different proficiency levels to simulate meditative states.

Result: The simulations generated behavioral patterns consistent with empirical evidence, successfully aligning first-person meditation experiences with objective neurophysiological data.

Conclusion: This computational approach bridges the subjective experience of meditation and its measurable effects on brain dynamics, providing insights into the neural mechanisms underlying focused-attention practices.

Abstract: Meditative expertise involves sustained attention, rapid recovery from distraction, and coordinated dynamics of large-scale brain networks. We present a computational phenomenology of focused-attention meditation traversing four attractor states: breath focus, mind-wandering, meta-awareness, and redirect attention. Within a dual-process active inference formulation, the model implements a three-layer nested Markov-blanket architecture: (L1) a high-dimensional physiological neuronal substrate modeled as a stochastic multivariate Ornstein--Uhlenbeck process over attentional Yeo networks; (L2) a low-dimensional generative model (System 1) that encodes latent mental content as thoughtseeds and evaluates autonomic action tendencies; and (L3) an agentic metacognitive monitor (System 2) that implements a Global Neuronal Workspace (GNW) capacity bottleneck to selectively gate these tendencies. In L3, meta-awareness functions as the GNW ignition signal, derived from policy-prior divergence and dynamically gated by direct competition between orchestrator and distractor thoughtseeds. Policy selection actively minimizes expected free energy, and L2 actions furnish descending predictions over network activity to close the enactive perception--action cycle. Training uses variational Expectation-Maximization (EM) across expert and novice phenotypes. Simulations reproduce behavior consistent with empirical observations and findings in contemplative neuroscience, providing a tractable link between first-person phenomenology and objective neurophysiological measures.

</details>


<div id='stat.ML'></div>

# stat.ML [[Back]](#toc)

### [418] [Generalized Neural Distributional Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14122)
*Natan Hilario da Silva,Vicente Garibay Cancho,Adriano Kamimura Suzuki*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: A framework called Generalized Neural Distributional Regression (GNDR) is proposed, combining deep neural networks and classical probability distributions for improved statistical modeling and uncertainty quantification in diverse datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of combining deep learning's powerful representational capacity with rigorous statistical properties of classical probability distributions, especially in contexts requiring uncertainty quantification.

Method: GNDR embeds neural networks into the parameter space of classical distributions and employs a two-step semi-parametric estimation procedure for reconciling deep architecture non-identifiability with maximum likelihood theory. Analytical Fisher Information matrices are extracted to facilitate uncertainty quantification.

Result: The GNDR framework demonstrated superior distributional calibration and versatility across applications like clinical count data, survival analysis, and age distributions derived from images.

Conclusion: This proposed methodology effectively bridges deep learning with classical statistical theory, enhancing its potential application in diverse and complex data scenarios. An open-source Python package, 'thetaflow,' implements this framework.

Abstract: We introduce the Generalized Neural Distributional Regression (GNDR) framework, which seamlessly embeds deep neural networks into the parameter space of classical probability distributions. To reconcile the inherent non-identifiability of deep architectures with maximum likelihood theory, we propose a two-step semi-parametric estimation procedure. By isolating the terminal prediction heads and treating the upstream network as a fixed, non-linear basis expansion, GNDR enables the extraction of analytical Fisher Information matrices. This facilitates rigorous uncertainty quantification, generating observation-specific confidence bands and tolerance intervals via the multivariate Delta method. We demonstrate the framework's versatility and superior distributional calibration across diverse data modalities, including overdispersed clinical counts, right-censored transcriptomic survival profiles under a mixture cure framework, and zero-truncated age distributions derived directly from unstructured facial images. The methodology is natively implemented in the open-source Python package \textit{thetaflow}.

</details>


### [419] [Operator-Informed Gaussian Processes for Complex Helmholtz Wavefields: From Synthetic Benchmarks to In Vivo Brain Elastography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14193)
*Boyuan Deng,Kshitiz Upadhyay,Michael Shields*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces a method to solve and infer complex-valued wave propagation problems using an extended version of Gaussian-process regression, focusing on uncertainty quantification and achieving competitive results in benchmark and brain imaging applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for inferring wavefields in dissipative media lack robust uncertainty quantification and primarily handle real-valued problems. There is a need for a probabilistic approach tailored for complex-valued Helmholtz problems.

Method: The authors extend Gaussian-process regression to complex-valued Helmholtz problems by reformulating the complex operator into a coupled real block. This allows the use of standard real-valued GP conditioning and includes a family of priors to enhance modeling capabilities.

Result: The method performs well on benchmark problems, competes effectively with traditional and neural-network-based approaches, and reconstructs magnetic resonance elastography brain data with high correlation. However, accuracy limitations and uncertainty calibration issues are identified.

Conclusion: The extended GP regression approach effectively handles complex-valued wavefields and provides credible uncertainty quantification. Future work should address uncertainty calibration to enhance the framework's reliability in real-world applications.

Abstract: The Helmholtz equation governs time-harmonic wave propagation, and in dissipative media a complex modulus renders its squared wavenumber $κ^2$ complex. Inferring such fields from sparse, noisy data calls for solvers that also quantify their own uncertainty. Physics-informed Gaussian-process (GP) regression supplies this by returning a posterior over the solution, yet operator-conditioned formulations have been developed almost exclusively for real-valued fields. We extend operator-informed GP regression to complex-valued Helmholtz problems by realifying the complex operator into an equivalent coupled real block, which enables inference with standard real-valued GP conditioning. The construction admits a family of priors, from a proper diagonal prior to coregionalized and multiscale variants, and conditions on PDE residuals and boundary traces. On benchmark problems in one to three dimensions, the solver is competitive with finite-difference and neural-network baselines at a far smaller interior-constraint budget. Unlike those deterministic baselines, it returns a posterior over the complex wavefield rather than a point estimate. Applied to \textit{in vivo} brain magnetic resonance elastography, a proper multiscale prior reconstructs the shear curl field to a correlation of $0.77$ with measurement, above a $0.75$ target. The gain arises from the multiscale kernel rather than from real--imaginary coupling. We further identify a low-frequency accuracy ceiling set by model mismatch and a posterior uncertainty that is not yet calibrated. Calibrated uncertainty therefore emerges as the central next step for probabilistic wavefield inference in dissipative media.

</details>


### [420] [Spectral Concentration and Recovery in Sparse High-Dimensional Random Geometric Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14304)
*Manuel Fernandez,Yizhe Zhu*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper examines sparse random geometric graphs formed by high-dimensional vector connections, improving spectral norm bounds and recovery guarantees under certain models and conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance understanding and analysis of high-dimensional sparse geometric graphs, especially in their connectivity, spectral properties, and latent geometry recovery.

Method: Utilizes orthogonal polynomial expansions, decoupling, and matrix concentration techniques to sharpen bounds and achieve recovery guarantees, avoiding traditional trace-moment approaches.

Result: Provides improved spectral norm estimations, latent geometry recovery, and exact recovery insights for both spherical and Gaussian distributed graph models.

Conclusion: The research offers refined bounds and recovery methodologies in sparse geometric graphs, achieving significant improvements in connectivity and latent geometry analysis.

Abstract: We study sparse random geometric graphs generated by connecting pairs of high-dimensional vectors whose inner product exceeds a threshold. The latent vectors are sampled either uniformly from the sphere or from a standard Gaussian distribution. Although every edge appears with probability $p$, the edges are dependent through their shared latent vectors. For the spherical model, at the connectivity scale $np=Ω(\log n)$, we prove $\|A-\mathbb E A\|=O\left(\sqrt{np\log n}+npτ\right)$, with high probability, where $τ$ is the cap threshold. This sharpens the spectral norm bound of Liu, Mohanty, Schramm, and Yang (2023) under weaker assumptions. An analogous result holds for the Gaussian model after removing the fluctuations of the vector norms, yielding improved global synchronization guarantees for the homogeneous Kuramoto model. We then recover the latent geometry from the leading eigenspace. When $np\gg\log n$, both the latent vector and relative Gram matrix errors vanish provided $d\ll np\log(1/p)/\log n$. The required lower dimension is only $d\gg\log(1/p)$ for the spherical model and $d\gg\log^2(1/p)\log n$ for the Gaussian model, improving the recovery guarantees of Li and Schramm (2023). Finally, we prove the first exact recovery result for the Gaussian mixture block model of Li and Schramm (2023). At the optimal connectivity scale $np=Ω(\log n)$, a polynomial-time semidefinite program exactly recovers all labels in a moderate-separation regime, whereas larger separation makes exact recovery impossible because isolated vertices appear with high probability. Our proofs combine orthogonal polynomial expansions, decoupling, and matrix concentration, avoiding the trace-moment arguments used in previous work.

</details>


### [421] [Optimal Self-Distillation for Rectified Flow via Linear Probing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14947)
*Saptarshi Roy,Debepsita Mukherjee,Pratik Patil*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper studies optimal self-distillation for rectified flow to improve teacher velocity fields using a mixture of true and teacher velocities, showing provable improvements and introducing a new tuning procedure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study addresses the challenges and opportunities of training generative models on self-generated signals, specifically focusing on whether a student model can effectively improve its teacher model in rectified flow settings.

Method: The paper derives a closed-form solution for the optimal mixing coefficient for self-distillation, proposes an affine path identity for ridge-regularized linear RF, and introduces a one-shot generalized cross-validation procedure to tune mixing weights without costly grid search.

Result: The proposed approach leads to provable reductions in velocity risk, enhanced mode recovery, and improved finite-step generation performance across Gaussian models, Gaussian mixtures, and image data.

Conclusion: Optimal self-distillation is a viable strategy for rectified flow applications, offering practical and theoretical improvements over teachers and pure distillation methods.

Abstract: Modern generative models are increasingly trained using model-generated signals, creating both opportunities for self-improvement and risks of collapse. We study optimal self-distillation (SD) for rectified flow (RF): given a suboptimal teacher velocity field, can a student trained on a mixture of true RF velocities and teacher velocities provably improve the teacher? For linear RF with ridge regularization on fixed interpolation pairs, we prove an exact affine path identity, derive the optimal mixing coefficient in closed form, and show strict improvement in integrated velocity risk whenever the teacher risk is nonstationary along the regularization path. The optimal coefficient obeys a sign rule: positive mixing corrects under-regularized teachers, while negative mixing corrects over-regularized teachers. We also give one-shot generalized cross-validation (GCV) and validation tuning procedure that avoids grid search over mixing weights and repeated refitting. Combining this theorem with RF Wasserstein convergence bounds, we show that optimal self-distillation improves the velocity estimation terms controlling continuous-time and finite-step generation error. Experiments with Gaussian models, Gaussian mixtures, and image data show that optimal self-distillation improves velocity risk, mode recovery, and finite-step generation relative to both the teacher and pure distillation.

</details>


### [422] [cGAP: Generalized Association Plots with HOMALS-Guided Heatmaps for Visualization of High-Dimensional Categorical Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.15018)
*Chun-houh Chen,Shun-Chuan Chang,Chiun-How Kao,Yi-Ju Lee,Shang-Ying Shiu,Yin-Jing Tien,ShengLi Tzeng,Han-Ming Wu*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces cGAP, a visualization framework that enhances interpretability for high-dimensional categorical data using HOMALS-embedded heatmaps and coordinated views.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visualization tools for high-dimensional categorical data are underdeveloped compared to those for continuous variables, and existing methods suffer from scaling issues, lack of connection to raw data, or prioritizing predictive accuracy over interpretability.

Method: cGAP uses Homogeneity Analysis (HOMALS) to embed data into a 3D Euclidean space and incorporates red-green-blue mappings for similitude, along with seriation algorithms for reordering matrices to reveal patterns, clusters, and structures.

Result: The framework provides coordinated views including heatmaps, proximity matrices, and seriation outputs, demonstrated through applications in diverse fields such as genetics, biomedicine, and social sciences.

Conclusion: cGAP offers an integrated visualization framework to investigate categorical datasets transparently, bridging the gap between derived visual patterns and the original data, applicable to various scientific domains.

Abstract: High-dimensional categorical data arise in genetics, biomedicine, and the social sciences, yet visualization tools for such data remain far less developed than those for continuous variables. Existing methods either scale poorly, rely heavily on low-dimensional displays detached from the original data matrix, or prioritize predictive accuracy over interpretability. To address this gap, we introduce categorical Generalized Association Plots (cGAP), a visualization framework for nominal, ordinal, and binary data that preserves the original data matrix while augmenting it with interpretable geometric structure. cGAP uses Homogeneity Analysis (HOMALS) to embed subjects and category levels in a three-dimensional Euclidean space and maps the embedding to red-green-blue coordinates so that similar patterns receive similar colors. The framework integrates three coordinated views: a HOMALS-guided heatmap of the raw data matrix, a subject proximity matrix, and a variable proximity matrix. Seriation algorithms are then used to reorder rows and columns to reveal coherent clusters, outliers, and local-to-global structure. We also derive barycentric traceability, projection-distortion, and contrast-preservation properties that clarify how embedding geometry is transferred to the display. We demonstrate the versatility of cGAP through applications to student-animal classification data, mammalian dentition profiles, mushroom records from the UCI Machine Learning Repository, and the Clusters of Orthologous Genes database. These examples show that cGAP supports transparent exploratory analysis by maintaining traceability between derived visual structure and the original categorical observations. cGAP provides a full-matrix, heatmap-based visualization environment for investigating complex categorical datasets across scientific domains.

</details>


### [423] [Subjective Risk Decomposition: A New View for Uncertainty Quantification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.15196)
*Raghad Alamri,Michele Caprio,Gavin Brown*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel perspective for uncertainty quantification (UQ), deriving uncertainty measures as consequences of higher-level modeling decisions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To unify and provide a common theoretical foundation for existing uncertainty measures by reframing them as derivatives of subjective risk decompositions, rather than standalone primitives.

Method: The authors use subjective risk decomposition based on strictly proper loss functions to derive and analyze uncertainty measures, and extend this framework to connect UQ with learning-theoretic concepts like excess risk, approximation error, and estimation error.

Result: The framework recovers classic information-theoretic uncertainty measures and unifies various uncertainty measures proposed in prior research, offering new insights into their theoretical underpinnings.

Conclusion: This work lays the groundwork for a comprehensive learning-theoretic framework for UQ, providing both theoretical clarity and practical methods for deriving uncertainty measures from modeling decisions.

Abstract: We present a novel viewpoint for uncertainty quantification. Uncertainty measures are not primitives, in need of axioms and argumentation, but instead consequences, of higher-level modelling decisions. We show how epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty measures can be derived via decomposition of a subjective risk, based on a strictly proper loss. Reverse cross-entropy provides a prominent example, where decomposition recovers the classic information-theoretic uncertainty terms. The same approach recovers numerous measures previously proposed across the UQ literature, providing them a common theoretical foundation. From a practical point of view, this suggests a new approach to UQ: given a modelling scenario and strictly proper loss, the corresponding epistemic and aleatoric terms are induced by the subjective-risk decomposition. We then extend our view to learning theory: we introduce and analyse subjective risk analogues of excess risk, approximation error, and estimation error, and identify the connections to UQ. We consider this a first step towards a full learning-theoretic framework for uncertainty quantification.

</details>


<div id='stat.CO'></div>

# stat.CO [[Back]](#toc)
