### [424] [Delocalization of bias in unadjusted Hamiltonian Monte Carlo and underdamped Langevin](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.15208)
*Yifan Chen,Xiaoou Cheng,Jonathan Niles-Weed,Jonathan Weare*

Main category: stat.CO

TL;DR: The paper extends the delocalization of bias theory to unadjusted Hamiltonian Monte Carlo and underdamped Langevin algorithms, showing that $O(\sqrt{K})$ integration steps control bias in $K$-dimensional marginals of high-dimensional distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To tackle the inherent bias of unadjusted samplers and reduce the iteration complexity associated with Metropolis-Hastings adjustment.

Method: The authors use a matrix-polynomial framework to address integration issues and demonstrate delocalization of bias for discrete-time integrators in high-dimensional distributions.

Result: Bias control for $K$-dimensional marginals was achieved with $O(\sqrt{K})$ steps, allowing for efficiency while considering weak or sparse variable interactions.

Conclusion: Delocalization of bias for unadjusted algorithms offers computational benefits, validating their efficiency in high-dimensional sampling scenarios.

Abstract: Unadjusted samplers such as unadjusted Hamiltonian Monte Carlo and underdamped Langevin are well-known to be biased. Metropolis--Hastings adjustment has been conventionally incorporated into Hamiltonian Monte Carlo to eliminate the bias. However, this adjustment can significantly increase the iteration complexity due to the small step size required for reasonable Metropolis acceptance rates. In this work, we extend the \emph{delocalization of bias} phenomenon, previously established for the overdamped Langevin algorithm, to these two unadjusted algorithms. We show that to control the $W_2$ bias of any $K$-dimensional marginal of a high-dimensional distribution, $O(\sqrt{K})$ integration steps suffice up to $\log d$ terms, assuming either weak or sparse interactions among variables. The discrete-time integrators here introduce technical difficulties beyond those of the overdamped setting, which we address through a broadly applicable matrix-polynomial framework that characterizes their propagators. Our result for the underdamped Langevin algorithm is valid for all large friction parameters, implying that the Leimkuhler-Matthews integrator for the overdamped Langevin dynamics also exhibits delocalization of bias.

</details>


<div id='cs.CE'></div>

# cs.CE [[Back]](#toc)

### [425] [One-Shot Generative Design for Disordered Metamaterials via Self-Organizing Neural Cellular Automata](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14475)
*Yujie Xiang,Liwei Wang*

Main category: cs.CE

TL;DR: The paper introduces a generative design framework using Neural Cellular Automata to effortlessly grow disordered metamaterials with diverse microstructures, enabling dynamic and versatile material properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Designing disordered microstructures holds great potential for achieving broader properties and performance, but current methods struggle due to limited expressiveness or dependence on large datasets.

Method: The proposed framework employs Neural Cellular Automata to learn local interaction rules from a single template, allowing the system to generate complex and adaptable disordered microstructures through dynamic growth.

Result: The framework successfully generates customizable microstructures that offer spatial property variations, demonstrated through application in mechanical cloaking with optimized heterogeneous distributions.

Conclusion: This innovative and data-efficient approach paves the way for designing versatile disordered materials, unlocking opportunities in applications like biomedical implants and soft robotics.

Abstract: Disordered metamaterials feature microstructures with inherent randomness and irregularity, enabling them to achieve broader property coverage and superior performance unavailable in their regular counterparts. Despite their promise, designing disordered microstructures is substantially harder than designing regular ones. Their design remains trapped between manual parameterizations with limited expressiveness, and generative AI that is data-hungry and struggles to generalize. To address these limitations, we propose a generative design framework based on Neural Cellular Automata that dynamically grows complex microstructures through learned local interaction rules, inspired by the self-organizing processes in natural materials. This framework requires only a single training template, yet accommodates diverse disordered microstructures and adapts to irregular domains and arbitrary discretizations. By manipulating the learned local rules, we can steer the growth process to generate microstructures unseen during training, providing control over orientation, anisotropy, and directional thickness without retraining. As a dynamic, local growth process, it naturally produces spatially varying microstructures that transition smoothly to enable location-specific mechanical properties. We demonstrate this in a multiscale mechanical cloaking design, where microstructures vary across the space to meet an optimized heterogeneous property distribution. Our design enables excellent cloaking performance without complicated post-processing and incompatible assembly common in existing methods. This data-efficient, generalizable approach opens access to previously intractable disordered materials for biomedical implants and soft robotics.

</details>


### [426] [Accounting for Hysteresis and Eddy Currents in Finite Element Simulations of Ferromagnetic Laminated Cores using a Recurrent Neural Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14321)
*Florent Purnode,Louis Denis,François Henrotte,Gilles Louppe,Christophe Geuzaine*

Main category: cs.CE

TL;DR: The paper introduces a recurrent neural network (RNN)-based surrogate model to simulate laminated-core materials in electrical machines. It achieves high accuracy while significantly reducing computational costs compared to traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for accurate modeling of laminated-core electrical machines is growing due to complex topologies and operating conditions. Current models either simplify the physics too much or are computationally impractical for design applications.

Method: A recurrent neural network is trained on artificial magnetic field sequences to mimic the behavior of laminated-core materials. It is then integrated into magnetodynamic finite element simulations based on a magnetic vector potential formulation.

Result: The RNN surrogate model mirrors the reference laminated-core model closely while reducing computational costs to approximately twice that of simpler anhysteretic simulations.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that RNN surrogate models can provide an efficient, accurate, and generalizable solution for simulating electromagnetic behaviors in electrical machines, making it feasible for integration into existing computational workflows.

Abstract: Incorporating hysteresis and eddy currents into finite element simulations of laminated-core electrical machines is computationally challenging. Resolving the fields inside the laminations at each integration point and at every nonlinear iteration leads to computational costs several orders of magnitude higher than anhysteretic simulations, making such approaches impractical for design applications. Conversely, simplified models accounting only for magnetic saturation are becoming increasingly inadequate as electrical machine topologies and operating conditions grow in complexity. In this context, machine learning surrogate modeling has emerged as a promising alternative, offering efficient and accurate approximations of complex electromagnetic behaviors. In this paper, a recurrent neural network is trained as a surrogate of a laminated-core material model for an isotropic laminated core, and is integrated into realistic two-dimensional magnetodynamic finite element simulations based on a magnetic vector potential formulation. The proposed approach achieves excellent agreement with the reference laminated-core model while limiting the computational cost to about twice that of an anhysteretic simulation. By training the recurrent neural network on a sufficiently diverse set of artificially generated magnetic field sequences designed to mimic those encountered in electrical machine simulations, the proposed approach can be readily applied across a wide range of finite element simulations. Furthermore, the trained surrogate model is provided as a standalone component that can be easily incorporated into existing computational frameworks. It is publicly available at https://gitlab.onelab.info/getdp/lamnet.

</details>


<div id='cs.GR'></div>

# cs.GR [[Back]](#toc)

### [427] [Instant NuRec: Feed-Forward 3D Gaussian Reconstruction for Driving Scene Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14203)
*NVIDIA,:,Jiahui Huang,Jiawei Ren,Michal Tyszkiewicz,Bjoern Haefner,Michael Shelley,Xin Kang,Seung Wook Kim,Ning Xu,Qi Wu,Janick Martinez Esturo,Shengyu Huang,Nick Schneider,Laura Leal-Taixe,Zan Gojcic,Sanja Fidler*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: The paper introduces Instant NuRec, a fast neural reconstruction model for generating simulatable 3D driving environments directly from short multi-view logs, significantly improving reconstruction speed and quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current neural simulation methods for autonomous driving are slow and require per-scene tuning, increasing complexity and limitations for realistic simulation.

Method: Instant NuRec utilizes a feed-forward neural architecture to transform multi-view driving logs into simulation-ready 3D Gaussian Splatting worlds in seconds. It integrates static and dynamic layers, sky cubemaps, camera corrections, and novel support for non-pinhole cameras.

Result: The model reconstructs multi-camera driving scenes in approximately 1.5 seconds, surpassing baseline performance by 2.01 dB in PSNR on the Waymo Open Dataset.

Conclusion: Instant NuRec presents a significant advancement in both speed and accuracy for neural 3D reconstruction, enabling seamless integration into existing simulation frameworks to support autonomous driving system development.

Abstract: 3D simulation platforms are critical for autonomous driving because they enable end-to-end policy evaluation, thereby reducing development costs and improving safety. In recent years, neural simulation has become predominant, with methods such as NuRec playing a central role; however, these methods remain relatively slow and typically require per-scene tuning. In this work, we present Instant NuRec, a feed-forward neural reconstruction model that turns a short multi-view driving log into a fully simulatable 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) world in a single forward pass. The model accepts multi-view input from a calibrated camera rig and emits a layered output consisting of static and dynamic 3DGS layers, a sky cubemap, and per-camera ISP corrections, while providing native support for non-pinhole camera models via 3DGUT. It reconstructs a 10-20-second multi-camera scene in roughly 1.5 seconds and achieves a PSNR on the Waymo Open Dataset that is 2.01 dB above the strongest evaluated baseline. Instant NuRec is deeply integrated into NuRec and is compatible with AlpaSim for closed-loop simulation.

</details>


<div id='cs.CY'></div>

# cs.CY [[Back]](#toc)

### [428] [Penny: Transition Network Analysis of Learner-Chatbot Interactions in Scaffolded EFL Writing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14575)
*Steve Woollaston,Brendan Flanagan,Yuko Toyokawa,Hiroaki Ogata*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: This study analyzes Japanese EFL learners’ interaction with an LLM-powered writing chatbot, uncovering key behavioral patterns and emphasizing the need for differentiated chatbot designs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative AI chatbots have the potential to reshape EFL writing by offering instant, personalized feedback, but their effectiveness depends on learner engagement, which remains underexplored.

Method: Transition Network Analysis was employed on data from 4,500 writing sessions and 21,000 interactions with the LLM-powered chatbot "Penny" to study temporal patterns of learner behaviors.

Result: There are two dominant behavioral loops: a "Revision Loop" for direct error correction and a "Chat Loop" for sustained dialogue. Proficiency levels significantly influence these interaction patterns, with high-proficiency learners engaging in deeper negotiation and lower-proficiency learners focusing on corrective cycles.

Conclusion: AI-assisted writing is a dynamic, dialogic process that necessitates differentiated designs in chatbots to support deeper cognitive engagement and tailored learning experiences.

Abstract: Generative AI chatbots promise to transform English as a Foreign Language (EFL) writing by providing immediate, personalised feedback. However, their pedagogical value depends on how learners engage with them - a process often treated as a "black box." This study uses Transition Network Analysis to model the temporal dynamics of Japanese EFL learners using "Penny," an LLM-powered writing chatbot. Analysis of over 4,500 writing sessions and 21,000 chatbot interactions reveals two dominant behavioural loops: a "Revision Loop," where feedback leads directly to successful error correction, and a "Chat Loop," where learners engage in sustained dialogue with the chatbot following feedback. Crucially, EFL proficiency significantly shapes interaction: high-proficiency learners engage more in open dialogue and negotiation with the chatbot, while low-proficiency learners rely more heavily on repetitive corrective feedback cycles. The findings demonstrate that AI-scaffolded writing is a non-linear, dialogic process and highlight the need for differentiated chatbot design to move beyond simple error correction and foster deeper cognitive engagement for all learners.

</details>


### [429] [Unsafe at any AUC: Unlearned Lessons from Sociotechnical Disasters for Responsible AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14353)
*Joshua A. Kroll,Andrew Smart,R. Stuart Geiger,Abigail Z. Jacobs*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The paper emphasizes that the risks of AI systems should be analyzed in their full sociotechnical context, focusing not just on individual components but on larger systems-level risks, using historical human-made catastrophes as lessons.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper is motivated by the need to address limitations in understanding and mitigating risks associated with AI systems, which are often underestimated or improperly analyzed at a systems level.

Method: The authors applied sociotechnical analysis and drew lessons from historical human-made catastrophic events, examining organizational and systemic failures to develop applicable methodologies for AI systems.

Result: The authors identified key factors contributing to failures in AI systems, including poor risk perception and communication, lack of responsibility traceability, and insufficient integration of social and organizational dynamics in engineering practices.

Conclusion: AI systems can significantly benefit from lessons learned in catastrophic events to develop more holistic and robust approaches to risk management and ensure responsibility, safety, and accountability.

Abstract: As automated decision-making and data-driven technologies pervade society and are used to manage consequential outcomes, understanding the technology's capabilities, limitations, and attendant risks in context requires analysis of full sociotechnical systems. Sociotechnical analysis of risks in highly complex systems provides clear lessons for the design and evaluation of AI systems, transcending a technical focus on reliable or "responsibly designed" components to understand risks at a systems level.
  Human-made catastrophes have been studied for decades because of the severity of these events: consider Chernobyl, Three Mile Island, Fukushima-Daiichi, Bhopal, the Challenger disaster. A common misconception is that these kinds of events are freak accidents, resulting from the inherently unforeseeable interactions in complex systems. Closer examination reveals that the risks and hazards were well-known beforehand but not acted upon due to social structural, political and economic factors.
  We outline several areas where the development and use of AI can benefit from learning these unlearned lessons: improved risk perception, communication, and analysis at the organizational level; traceability of requirements and responsibilities; and holistic approaches to responsibility and safety that include social and organizational dynamics as first-order engineering concerns. For each area, we offer concrete unlearned lessons and exemplify how they led to failure in prior accidents as well as examples of how these lessons remain unlearned for modern computing systems, particularly AI.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.QM'></div>

# q-bio.QM [[Back]](#toc)

### [430] [A vision foundation model for single-cell biology via spatial gene cartography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14163)
*Ridvan Yesiloglu,Sakib Mostafa,James Zou,Ash Alizadeh,Jiajun Wu,Lei Xing,Ehsan Adeli,Md Tauhidul Islam*

Main category: q-bio.QM

TL;DR: scVision, a vision foundation model, treats single-cell data as images by creating a spatial layout for genes and uses a vision transformer for cell type annotation and gene program recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current single-cell foundation models disregard gene relationships and expression magnitude by representing cells as sequences of tokens.

Method: scVision generates continuous 'images' of cells using optimal transport to spatially organize co-expressed genes, then trains a vision transformer on these images through masked image modeling.

Result: scVision outperforms existing models in zero-shot cell-type annotation and gene program recovery, while preserving biological structure better than other methods.

Conclusion: By treating single-cell data as a vision problem, scVision successfully connects single-cell representation learning with advanced computer vision techniques.

Abstract: Most single-cell foundation models are adapted from language models, representing each cell as a sequence of gene tokens. This discards the relationships among genes and often the magnitude of their expression. We present scVision, a vision foundation model that instead renders each cell as a continuous image. Using optimal transport, it places genes at fixed positions on a single shared, pan-tissue layout so that co-expressed genes become spatial neighbours, turning a transcriptome into an image in which gene programs appear as local texture. We pretrain a vision transformer by masked image modelling on 72 million human cells and use the frozen encoder with no fine-tuning. In zero-shot evaluations on six independent, held-out studies, scVision is the most accurate cell-type annotator and recovers gene programs without supervision, ahead of existing foundation models and classical baselines; on multi-study integration it matches the strongest token-based model while conserving the most biological structure, without ever seeing a batch label. Permuting the gene layout with the network fixed sharply lowers accuracy, more than removing the vision transformer itself, showing that biologically meaningful position, not the network, carries the signal. By preserving expression magnitude and gene relationships, scVision reframes single-cell representation learning as a vision problem, connecting it to the mature methods of computer vision.

</details>


<div id='math.OC'></div>

# math.OC [[Back]](#toc)

### [431] [Tamed Stochastic Gradient Hamiltonian Monte Carlo](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14862)
*Zhuoran Wang,Ying Zhang*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: The paper proposes tamed stochastic gradient Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (tSGHMC) for handling superlinearly growing stochastic gradients, establishing strong theoretical guarantees and demonstrating practical effectiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current optimization algorithms struggle with sampling and optimization problems involving superlinearly growing stochastic gradients, necessitating robust and efficient approaches.

Method: The authors develop the tamed stochastic gradient Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (tSGHMC) algorithm, proving its convergence rate and deriving error bounds under certain mathematical conditions. They validate its effectiveness through both theoretical analysis and practical applications.

Result: tSGHMC achieves a convergence rate of 1/4 in Wasserstein-2 distance, offers reduced root mean square error and expected excess risk, and outperforms its first-order alternative in both synthetic and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: tSGHMC is a theoretically sound and practically superior algorithm for addressing problems with superlinearly growing stochastic gradients, making it suitable for optimization and sampling tasks.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose a novel tamed stochastic gradient Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (tSGHMC) algorithm for sampling and stochastic optimization problems with superlinearly growing stochastic gradients. Under a certain continuity in average condition and a strong convexity condition, we establish a non-asymptotic error bound in Wasserstein-2 distance for tSGHMC with the rate of convergence equal to $1/4$. Then, we derive an upper estimate for the associated expected excess risk, which provides a theoretical guarantee for the performance of tSGHMC. To illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm, we apply tSGHMC to practical examples, including a newsvendor problem and a Conditional Value-at-Risk minimization problem, using synthetic and real-world datasets. Numerical results support our theoretical findings. Furthermore, we compare tSGHMC with its first-order counterpart, namely, the tamed unadjusted stochastic Langevin algorithm. Simulation results demonstrate that tSGHMC achieves lower root mean square error and expected excess risk across a range of tasks.

</details>


<div id='physics.ed-ph'></div>

# physics.ed-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [432] [Assessing AI in Introductory Physics Problem Solving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14303)
*Amir Bralin,N. Sanjay Rebello*

Main category: physics.ed-ph

TL;DR: The study evaluates OpenAI's model o4-mini on undergraduate physics problems and reports a 90% overall accuracy, with variations based on the problem's representation and difficulty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate the problem-solving abilities of reasoning-scaling models like OpenAI's o4-mini in the domain of undergraduate physics.

Method: The authors evaluated the o4-mini model on traditional physics problems from Halliday and Resnick's textbook, analyzing accuracy across problem representations (text-only versus text-and-image) and difficulty levels.

Result: The model achieved 90% overall accuracy, performing best on text-only problems (96%) but less effectively on text-and-image problems (79%). Performance also declined with increasing problem difficulty.

Conclusion: While state-of-the-art models like o4-mini demonstrate high competence in solving introductory physics problems, challenges persist in handling more complex modalities and higher difficulty levels.

Abstract: Reasoning or inference-scaling models are the new generation of Large Language Models (LLMs) capable of complex problem solving. To investigate their problem-solving capability in physics, we evaluated model o4-mini by OpenAI on solving traditional, end-of-chapter problems from Halliday and Resnick's "Fundamentals of Physics," spanning core topics in the undergraduate physics curriculum. Performance was analyzed across modality and problem difficulty. The model solved the problems with overall accuracy of about 90%, but performance depended strongly on representation: accuracy was much higher on text-only problems (96%) than on problems requiring coordinated interpretation of text and images (79%). Accuracy also declined significantly as the problem difficulty increased from low to medium to high. These results show that state-of-the-art LLMs can solve much of the standard introductory physics problems, but that their performance remains uneven and constrained by problem modality and problem difficulty.

</details>


<div id='eess.IV'></div>

# eess.IV [[Back]](#toc)

### [433] [OvAi Focus: AI-based Multi-class Segmentation of Functional Ovaries and Adnexal Masses in Gynecological Ultrasound](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14179)
*Niccolò Tallone,Francesca Salis,Pio Raffaele Fina,Roberta Massobrio,Rosilari Bellacosa Marotti,Daniele Conti,Luca Fuso,Luca Mariani,Annamaria Ferrero,Alessandro Arena,Stefano Cosma,Dan Grisaru,Angelo Lacalandra,Renato Seracchioli,Marianna Roccio,Federica Gerace*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: OvAi Focus, an AI-based tool, improves segmentation of ovarian masses and ovaries in ultrasound images, achieving superior performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate segmentation of ovarian structures in ultrasound is challenging due to operator variability and morphological complexity, necessitating more reliable tools.

Method: The paper introduces OvAi Focus, an AI-based semantic segmentation tool, trained and validated on a multicenter dataset of 1,081 adult women from six centers in Italy and Israel.

Result: The system achieved high DICE scores of 0.87 (complete lesion), 0.85 (cystic), 0.68 (solid), and 0.62 (functional ovary), demonstrating performance at or above state-of-the-art levels across various clinical settings.

Conclusion: OvAi Focus is a robust AI-driven solution for ovarian and adnexal mass segmentation, overcoming challenges in operator variability and morphological complexity.

Abstract: Ovarian cancer is the deadliest gynecological malignancy; accurate and objective segmentation of adnexal masses and functional ovaries in ultrasound (US) remains challenging due to operator variability and morphological complexity. We present OvAi Focus (SynDiag s.r.l., Italy), a stand-alone AI software medical device that performs multi-class semantic segmentation of functional ovaries and adnexal masses, distinguishing cystic from solid components. The system was trained and independently validated on a multicenter dataset of 1,081 adult women from 6 centers across Italy and Israel. Segmentation achieved DICE scores of 0.87 (complete lesion), 0.85 (cystic), 0.68 (solid), and 0.62 (functional ovary), in line with or superior to state-of-the-art approaches across heterogeneous acquisition settings.

</details>


### [434] [ViPSAM: Visual Prompting Medical Image Segmentation Using Segment Anything Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14328)
*San Lee,Nalee Kim,Jeong Il Yu,Hee Chul Park,Boah Kim*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: ViPSAM, a new method for lesion segmentation in proton therapy planning, utilizes cross-modality visual prompts for better non-contrast image segmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current challenges exist in accurately segmenting lesions on non-contrast CT (NCCT) scans due to low lesion-to-background contrast, while learning-based methods face limitations in addressing this issue.

Method: The method, ViPSAM, integrates complementary information from contrast-enhanced images into NCCT lesion segmentation using a visual prompting framework, visual prompt encoder, cross-modality attention, and an adapted mask decoder.

Result: Experimental analysis on liver lesion segmentation in proton therapy shows that ViPSAM surpasses U-Net and SAM-based techniques in accuracy and robustness.

Conclusion: Incorporating cross-modality visual information enhances lesion segmentation quality for non-contrast images, making ViPSAM a promising tool for more accurate proton therapy planning.

Abstract: In proton therapy planning, respiratory-gated non-contrast CT (NCCT) is commonly used for lesion segmentation; however, accurate delineation remains challenging due to low lesion-to-background contrast. Although learning-based methods have shown strong performance, they often struggle with non-contrast image segmentation. Inspired by clinical practice, where contrast-enhanced MRI is referenced to delineate lesions on NCCT, we propose ViPSAM, a visual prompting framework that leverages complementary cross-modality information. Built upon the Segment Anything Model (SAM), ViPSAM introduces a visual prompt encoder to extract guidance features from contrast-enhanced images and a visual-guided cross-attention module to integrate non-contrast and contrast-enhanced features, thereby enhancing lesion-relevant representations in low-contrast regions. The mask decoder is further adapted in a parameter-efficient manner to utilize visual prompts effectively. We evaluate the proposed method on liver lesion segmentation using NCCT acquired for proton therapy. Experimental results demonstrate that ViPSAM outperforms representative U-Net- and SAM-based methods, indicating that cross-modality visual prompting enables more robust and accurate segmentation in non-contrast images.

</details>


### [435] [ESAR: Event-Based Synthetic Aperture Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.15073)
*Harbir Antil,Daniel Blauvelt,David Sayre*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a novel approach for event-based imaging by formulating it as a synthetic-aperture inverse problem in a static ground-domain log-radiance field.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Event cameras capture changes in log-radiance rather than conventional images, providing a unique challenge to reconstruct spatial scenes effectively. Existing methods often aim to reconstruct pixel-time volumes, but the authors aim to focus on recovering static spatial scenes by imposing geometric constraints.

Method: The authors model the problem as an inverse problem where latent pixel-time volumes are mapped into a static scene using a projection, $P$. Aggregating events leads to a linearized model, and the recovery relies on regularized inversion techniques to handle the ill-conditioned nature of the composite operator $AP$.

Result: Experiments using simulated data and real Falcon Neuro event data demonstrate that the proposed formulation effectively reconstructs coherent large-scale spatial structures while suppressing fine-scale textures. It outperforms baseline methods in maintaining spatial coherence.

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates that shifting the focus to static scene reconstruction using synthetic-aperture geometry offers advantages in event-based imaging. This approach effectively recovers large-scale structures and reduces noise while addressing the challenges of traditional reconstructions.

Abstract: Event cameras report asynchronous polarity events when changes in log--radiance exceed a fixed contrast threshold, producing signed temporal contrast measurements rather than conventional image frames. We formulate monocular event-based imaging as a synthetic-aperture inverse problem for a static ground-domain log--radiance field $θ\in \mathbb{R}^{N_g}$. Instead of reconstructing a latent pixel-time volume $v \in \mathbb{R}^{N_pN_t}$, we impose the geometric relation $v=Pθ$, where $P$ maps the fixed scene into motion-dependent latent views. Aggregating events over finite time intervals gives the linearized model \[
  APθ= b+η, \] where $A$ is a temporal differencing operator, $b$ contains signed binned event counts, and $η$ represents measurement and modeling errors. This decomposition exposes a synthetic-aperture structure: under near-nadir motion, successive projections are approximately shifted views of a common scene, while the composite operator $AP$ remains ill-conditioned because it combines spatial averaging with temporal differencing. We therefore use regularized inversion to recover $θ$. Numerical experiments on simulated data and real near-nadir Falcon Neuro event data show that the proposed $θ$-based formulation recovers coherent large-scale spatial structure, relative to dynamic latent-image and learned event-reconstruction baselines, while suppressing fine-scale texture.

</details>


### [436] [Domain Adaptation of Mismatched Proximal Denoiser for Plug-and-Play Image Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14894)
*Guixian Xu,Jinglai Li,Junqi Tang*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: The paper extends the plug-and-play proximal gradient descent (PnP-PGD) framework by addressing the stability of denoisers under domain mismatches, introducing "proximal mismatch" as a key challenge, and proposing an adaptation method to improve performance in domain-shift scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many image reconstruction methods deploy denoisers in domains they were not originally trained for, leading to potential mismatches that can affect performance. The paper aims to formalize and resolve this issue by quantifying and mitigating the effects of such mismatches in PnP-PGD.

Method: The researchers propose the concept of 'proximal mismatch,' which measures the deviation between the deployed and true proximal maps, and introduce a stationarity bound analysis with a decay rate of \( \mathcal{O}(1/K) \). They recommend proximal matching-based adaptation to mitigate the introduced errors, extending analyses to learned proximal networks and gradient-step denoisers.

Result: Their experiments demonstrate that adapting denoisers through proximal matching significantly enhances image reconstruction quality, particularly in scenarios under substantial domain shifts and limited adaptation data.

Conclusion: The proposed proximal matching approach offers a theoretically motivated and practically effective solution to address domain mismatches in PnP-PGD, outperforming traditional MSE-based adaptation in improving image reconstruction quality.

Abstract: Plug-and-play proximal gradient descent (PnP-PGD) enables flexible image reconstruction by using denoisers as implicit priors. In practice, these denoisers are often deployed outside their training domains. Existing analyses establish convergence under structural assumptions on the deployed denoiser, such as requiring it to be a proximal map or a contraction. However, they do not measure how domain mismatch affects convergence of PnP-PGD. We define this effect as \emph{proximal mismatch}: the discrepancy between a deployed denoiser $\widehat{\mathsf D}$ and a target-domain reference map $\mathsf D_\star=\operatorname{prox}_{R_\star}$ associated with the underlying regularizer $R_\star$. Under this mismatch, each denoising update becomes an inexact proximal step for the target objective. We further derive a stationarity bound that decays at a rate of $\mathcal{O}(1/K)$, with an additive term proportional to the average squared proximal mismatch. This result motivates adaptation via proximal matching rather than MSE-based adaptation alone. We study this approach with two established denoiser families: learned proximal networks and gradient-step denoisers. Experiments on Gaussian deblurring and super-resolution under substantial domain shift show that proximal matching adaptation improves reconstruction quality significantly over MSE-based adaptation, yielding the largest numerical gains in the few-shot regime.

</details>


<div id='cs.ET'></div>

# cs.ET [[Back]](#toc)

### [437] [Global drivers and barriers to the public acceptance of autonomous vehicles: Evidence from 17 countries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14436)
*Antonios Saravanos*

Main category: cs.ET

TL;DR: The study explored global public acceptance of Level 3 automated cars using data from 18,603 respondents across 17 countries, finding that tangible benefits, social endorsement, and enjoyment were primary predictors of acceptance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address a gap in research on public acceptance of Level 3 automated cars, especially outside of European contexts, and to identify factors influencing this acceptance globally.

Method: The study used the UTAUT2 framework and a structural equation model to analyze data from 18,603 respondents across multiple continents from the L3Pilot Global User Acceptance Survey.

Result: Performance expectancy, social influence, and hedonic motivation were the primary drivers of acceptance, with weaker roles for effort expectancy and facilitating conditions. Demographic factors like age and gender had limited predictive power.

Conclusion: Acceptance of Level 3 automated cars is primarily influenced by perceptions of their usefulness, social approval, and enjoyability, rather than demographic variables or ease-of-use considerations.

Abstract: This study investigated the public acceptance of Society of Automotive Engineers Level 3 conditionally automated cars, which can self-drive under certain specified conditions but require the human driver to remain ready to resume control when requested. Previous Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology 2 (UTAUT2)-based research has focused mainly on European samples, and so it is still unclear whether the same factors shape acceptance across broader world regions. This knowledge gap was addressed using the L3Pilot Global User Acceptance Survey. From an original dataset of 18,631 respondents, the final analytic sample comprised 18,603 respondents from 17 countries across Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, and South America. The data were analyzed using a UTAUT2-based structural equation model to examine how performance expectancy, effort expectancy, social influence, facilitating conditions, and hedonic motivation shape the intention to use Level 3 cars. The model showed strong explanatory power. Across the analytic sample, the intention to use Level 3 cars was driven mainly by performance expectancy, social influence, and hedonic motivation. Effort expectancy and facilitating conditions also contributed, but they played smaller direct roles. Age, gender, and previous experience with advanced driver assistance systems were statistically significant, but comparatively weak predictors. Overall, the findings suggest that the acceptance of Level 3 automated cars depends less on demographic characteristics or ease-of-use concerns and more on whether people see the technology as useful, socially supported, and enjoyable to use.

</details>


<div id='cs.IR'></div>

# cs.IR [[Back]](#toc)

### [438] [Does generative AI supersede supervised XMLC? A Benchmark Study on Automated Subject Indexing with German Scientific Literature](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14882)
*Maximilian Kähler,Katja Konermann,Lisa Kluge,Markus Schumacher*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The study explores automated subject indexing for German scientific literature using Extreme Multi-Label Classification (XMLC) with supervised methods combined with baseline and generative approaches. Transformer-based features perform best in general relevance metrics, while generative LLM methods excel in long-tail vocabulary tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper investigates improving automated subject indexing for libraries, focusing on handling large vocabularies efficiently while maintaining high accuracy and relevance.

Method: Supervised XMLC methods, classical lexical matching, and recently developed LLM-based methods are applied and benchmarked using German National Library datasets.

Result: Transformer-based XMLC algorithms perform best in binary relevance metrics, while LLM-based generative methods show superior performance in graded relevance and long-tail subject vocabulary.

Conclusion: Generative LLMs present a promising future direction for automated library subject indexing, especially in tackling long-tail vocabulary challenges.

Abstract: With a large controlled vocabulary as the label set, the task of automated subject indexing in a library can be understood as a multi-label classification task. If the set of subject terms is large, the problem fits the Extreme Multi-Label Classification (XMLC) objective. In this study, we apply a selection of specialised supervised XMLC methods to the test case of subject indexing contemporary German scientific literature, collected at the German National Library (DNB). We contrast these results by including a classical lexical matching baseline and three of our own recently developed LLM-based methods into the benchmark. Algorithms are evaluated and compared in several metrics. This includes binary relevance comparisons with previously indexed material, as well as graded relevance ratings by professional subject librarians. A challenge for all methods is to reliably make suggestions from the long tail of the subject vocabulary. We find that supervised XMLC algorithms relying on transformer-based dense features give best results in terms of overall binary relevance metrics. However, focusing on graded relevance and performance in the long tail of our subject vocabulary, the LLM-based generative methods give better results, making them a promising alternative for future productive use.

</details>


### [439] [Deep-learning Causal Retrieval Optimization for Efficient e-commerce Distribution in Pinterest](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14161)
*Junpeng Hou,XianXing Zhang,Sai Xiao,Derek Cheng,Darren Reger,Olafur Gudmundsson,Mehdi Ben Ayed,Zhiqing Rao,Huizhong Duan*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: This study proposes a deep multi-task model to optimize shopping triggers on Pinterest, reducing unnecessary triggers while improving user engagement and infrastructure efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve user experience and engagement on Pinterest by providing shopping content that is helpful without being distracting.

Method: The paper introduces a causal decision framework using a deep multi-task model to predict outcomes and uplift of multiple events. The model incorporates doubly-robust pseudo-outcomes and is trained with calibrated losses. Data from randomized logging ensures counterfactual coverage, and a linear-time offline replay is used for policy impact evaluation and threshold selection.

Result: The implemented system reduced shopping triggers by up to 85%, increased total sessions by 0.26% and Pin saves by 1.10%, while achieving significant infrastructure savings.

Conclusion: The work presents a practical solution for early-retrieval optimization in cascading recommendation systems, balancing exploration, cost, and user intent effectively at scale.

Abstract: Pinterest is where people turn inspiration into action as users browse ideas, then take steps toward realization, often by discovering shoppable content. To support this journey, we must distribute commerce content when it helps, not when it distracts. We frame this as a causal decision of triggering shopping candidate generators in early retrieval and deploy a production system at Pinterest that learns personalized and contextualized triggering policies. A deep multi-task model jointly predicts outcomes and uplift of multiple events, trained with a doubly-robust pseudo-outcome alongside calibrated outcome losses for stable, single-robust uplift learning. A randomized data logging supplies counterfactual coverage, and the model is evaluated by both regular and reverse metrics for full assessment. A linear-time offline replay is designed to select thresholds and forecast policy impact with extremely high consistency with online results. For productionization, the model runs in parallel with remote retrieval calls without end-to-end latency regression. At web scale, we cut shopping triggers by up to 85% while holding key shopping sessions neutral, improving important total sessions (+0.26%) and Pin saves (+1.10%), with significant infrastructure savings. By unifying deep causal learning with reliable offline replay and demonstrating production-grade deployment, this work provides a generally practical recipe for early-retrieval optimizations in modern cascading recommenders beyond shopping, aligning exploration and cost with user intent at scale.

</details>


### [440] [Bridge Evidence: Static Retrieval Utility Does Not Predict Causal Utility in Multi-Step Agentic Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.15253)
*Debayan Mukhopadhyay,Utshab Kumar Ghosh,Shubham Chatterjee*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces Counterfactual Trajectory Utility (CTU) to measure relevance in agentic retrieval systems, showing that traditional methods of evaluating retrieval usefulness (Static RAG Utility, SRU) miss a class of documents, called bridge documents, which aid a search agent indirectly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current retrieval systems are static and fail to account for how documents might contribute to iterative reasoning by language models acting as search agents. This creates a need to measure relevance beyond static one-turn questions and answers.

Method: The authors use a ReAct-style agent on HotpotQA questions to replay queries, deleting individual documents mid-trajectory to evaluate their effect on performance. A Counterfactual Trajectory Utility (CTU) score is introduced based on the change in outcomes (answer quality, next query quality, turn count). They compare CTU to Static RAG Utility (SRU), analyze bridge documents, and explore the role of discriminative entities using Observable Entity Relevance (OER).

Result: CTU shows that 27.2% of the documents classified as irrelevant in static evaluations (SRU) are actually bridge documents, critical for agent progress when considering iterative multi-turn search. A statistically significant disconnect exists between CTU and SRU.

Conclusion: Evaluating document usefulness in agentic retrieval systems requires new metrics like CTU that account for their dynamic contributions across search trajectories. Static relevance and usefulness diverge, emphasizing the need to rethink search optimization strategies for agentic reasoning.

Abstract: Retrieval systems are trained and evaluated on a static idea of usefulness: hand a document and a question to a reader model, see whether the answer improves, and score the document accordingly. The idea holds up when a document is read on its own. It breaks when a language model works as a search agent, issuing several queries and reasoning across turns, because a document can matter for what it lets the agent do next rather than for what it says about the current question.
  We measure that gap rather than argue it. Using a ReAct style agent over HotpotQA, we replay 1000 development questions and, for every document the agent read, delete it and re-run the rest of the trajectory from that point. Comparing the original run against its counterfactual gives a Counterfactual Trajectory Utility (CTU) score from three deltas: final answer quality, next query retrieval quality, and turn count. Crossing CTU against Static RAG Utility (SRU) over 23,322 document observations, the two are close to statistically independent (Spearman rho = -0.026). Roughly a third of the documents the agent reads are causally load bearing while looking useless to a static reader; we call these bridge documents. The pattern survives when the reader based axis is swapped for a BM25 and cross encoder proxy, giving a bridge cell of 27.2% on an evenly spread axis.
  A second experiment pins down the mechanism. Using the Observable Entity Relevance (OER) measure from prior work, entities that discriminate relevant from non-relevant candidates appear in the agent's next query 4.02 times more often than entities found only in non-relevant documents (6.1% vs 1.5%, n = 227,139). A bridge document earns its keep by handing the agent a discriminative entity that redirects the search. Static relevance and causal usefulness are different quantities in agentic retrieval, and optimizing the first does not deliver the second.

</details>


<div id='cs.HC'></div>

# cs.HC [[Back]](#toc)

### [441] [Memory-Driven Self-Disclosure and Relational Turning Points: A Longitudinal Multimodal Study of Human-AI Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14593)
*Ryuichi Sumida,Mao Saeki,Masaki Eguchi,Sadahiro Yoshikawa,Koji Inoue,Tatsuya Kawahara,Yoichi Matsuyama*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper presents a study of how human-AI relationships evolve over time by analyzing interactions with a memory-augmented conversational agent. Findings highlight the importance of perceived memory, conversational quality, and discrete interaction turning points.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to understand how repeated interactions with conversational AI systems develop into sustained relationships over time.

Method: A longitudinal study involving 24 participants interacting with a memory-augmented conversational agent across 10 sessions. Researchers measured relational constructs such as familiarity, self-disclosure, perceived memory, conversational quality, and enjoyment after each session.

Result: The study found that conversational quality influences immediate enjoyment, but perceived memory strongly predicts long-term relationship dynamics. Relationships develop both gradually and via abrupt turning points detected through multimodal behavior.

Conclusion: Human-AI relationships are shaped by a combination of gradual changes and significant interaction turning points, with perceived memory playing a significant mediating role in fostering engagement and connection.

Abstract: As conversational AI systems are designed for repeated use, a central question is how a series of interactions becomes a relationship. We present a longitudinal multimodal study of a memory-augmented conversational agent (24 participants x 10 sessions), in which participants rated five relational constructs -- familiarity, self-disclosure, perceived memory, conversational quality, and enjoyment -- after each session. Two complementary dynamics emerge. First, conversational quality strongly shapes how enjoyable a session feels in the moment but does not carry forward across sessions, whereas perceived memory is relationally conditioned -- predicted by prior relational state rather than reflecting system capability alone -- and it shapes later enjoyment indirectly, via subsequent self-disclosure. Second, relationships are punctuated by discrete turning points -- crashes and surges -- that are partially traceable in multimodal behavior and open different intervention windows: surges are more behaviorally detectable in the moment, enjoyment surges persist more reliably than enjoyment crashes recover, and some crashes are better forecast from person-specific behavioral drift than detected after they have already occurred. Together, the findings suggest that longitudinal human-AI relationships are built through both slow accumulation and abrupt turning points.

</details>


### [442] ["Trust Junk" Leads to Unjustified Support for Highly Discriminatory Predictive Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14152)
*Michael Correll,Lucy Havens,Mahsan Nourani*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The study shows that accurate but irrelevant data in explainable AI visualizations can lead to unwarranted trust in discriminatory models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To analyze if accurate yet irrelevant data in XAI visualizations can create unjustified trust or positive beliefs about discriminatory models.

Method: Crowdsourced study evaluating the impact of irrelevant yet accurate data in model explanations on user trust and beliefs.

Result: People formed unjustified trust and positive beliefs about models, even when those models were discriminatory and unfair.

Conclusion: XAI designers must account for the rhetorical effects of their visualizations to avoid fostering unwarranted trust in models.

Abstract: The persuasive power of data visualizations can go awry: for instance, in an explainable AI (XAI) context, visualizations can produce over-trust of predictive models. In this paper, we use a crowdsourced study to show that providing accurate (but superfluous or irrelevant) data in a model explanation can, in fact, result in unjustified trust and other positive beliefs about a model, even when the model is patently discriminatory and unfair. Our results suggest that XAI designers and developers need to consider the implicit or explicit rhetorics of their work, and beware of the potential of visualizations to imbue models with unearned trust.

</details>


### [443] [Skeleton: Visual Authoring of Non-visual Data Experiences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14579)
*Frank Elavsky,Chieri Nnadozie,Lucas Nadolskis,Patrick Carrington,Dominik Moritz*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper introduces tools and techniques to help sighted practitioners design accessible data visualizations by visualizing and manipulating navigation structures, significantly enhancing their design process.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sighted practitioners currently build navigation structures for accessibility without visual feedback, which limits their ability to assess and improve the quality of non-visual user experiences.

Method: The authors conducted longitudinal co-design with practitioners and introduced tools like an Inspector for visualizing navigation graphs and Skeleton, a direct-manipulation authoring environment for accessible navigation design.

Result: Practitioners using these tools gained new insights into accessible design, reconsidered visualization architectures, addressed more input modalities, and shifted their mindset about accessibility from compliance to creative design.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates the value of visual representations in improving the development of accessible design, fostering a new paradigm in how practitioners approach accessibility challenges.

Abstract: When sighted practitioners author accessible data visualizations, they build navigation structures (the nodes, edges, and input bindings that govern how assistive technologies traverse an interface) entirely in code, with no visual representation. Without a representation to react to, practitioners cannot develop judgment about what makes navigation good or bad, and the quality ceiling of non-visual experiences is set by the absence of a feedback loop. We address this problem through longitudinal co-design with practitioners across cartography, design systems, and open-source visualization, and make three contributions. First, we introduce an Inspector that renders navigation graphs as interactive node-link diagrams, and a Dimensions API that expresses navigation in terms of data dimensions rather than explicit graph construction. Second we present Skeleton, a direct-manipulation authoring environment in which the properties of an accessible navigation structure are translated into visual representations authors can observe and manipulate. Key techniques include a dual-view editor that simultaneously shows the system's navigation model and the end user's spatial experience, a scaffolding engine that automates spatial node placement by repurposing a visualization rendering pipeline, a live label-template editor with real-time screen-reader-output preview, and a testing mode that makes traversal sequence visually trackable. Third, we evaluate Skeleton through an in-situ study with 8 practitioners across visualization design, engineering, and research. Making navigation structure visible changed how practitioners engaged with accessible design: they reconsidered the architecture of their own visualizations, attended to a broader range of input modalities, and shifted from treating accessibility as a compliance task to treating it as a design problem. (abstract shortened for arxiv)

</details>


### [444] [When AI Blurs the Boundaries of Contribution: An Empirical Study of Authorship Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.15006)
*Célina Treuillier,Denis Lalanne*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper examines how users perceive their own authorship when interacting with AI, revealing variability based on AI use frequency. Heavy AI users misjudge their authorship more than infrequent users.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding how interactions with generative AI impact users' perception of content authorship and its implications on learning and responsibility.

Method: The study employs the CoAuthor dataset to analyze variability in authorship calibration among users and its connection to AI usage frequency.

Result: Heavy AI users tend to misjudge their authorship, while infrequent users show better authorship calibration. Miscalibration could affect learning outcomes.

Conclusion: Promoting authorship calibration is crucial for responsible AI use in educational contexts, ensuring meaningful integration and positive learning strategies.

Abstract: The broad adoption of Artificial Intelligence (AI), especially Generative AI, raises pressing questions about how users interact with these systems to produce new content. In this paper, we introduce the concept of authorship calibration, defined as users awareness of their actual authorship when interacting with AI. Using the CoAuthor dataset, we empirically examine how authorship calibration varies across users and how it relates to their frequency of AI use. Our results reveal high variability: users relying heavily on AI tend to misjudge their authorship, whereas those using AI less frequently exhibit more accurate authorship calibration. These findings suggest that AI can obscure users perception of their own authorship. In learning contexts, miscalibration can affect metacognitive monitoring and learning strategies, ultimately impacting learning outcomes. Fostering authorship calibration then appears essential for promoting responsible and educationally meaningful AI integration.

</details>


<div id='cs.SD'></div>

# cs.SD [[Back]](#toc)

### [445] [ITGPT: A Transformer Based Architecture for the Generation of Dance Dance Revolution and In the Groove Charts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14148)
*Miguel O'Malley*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper introduces ITGPT, a transformer-based model for generating dance charts for rhythm games like DDR/ITG, improving accuracy and efficiency compared to previous methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The manual creation of dance charts for rhythm games is labor-intensive and challenging, driving the need for automation.

Method: The paper proposes ITGPT, a novel architecture utilizing transformers for automating dance chart generation.

Result: ITGPT improves generation accuracy and computational efficiency significantly compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: ITGPT is a promising approach for automating dance chart generation, addressing manual creation challenges and enhancing performance and cost-effectiveness.

Abstract: Dance Dance Revolution and In the Groove are rhythm games consisting of songs and accompanying choreography, referred to as charts. Players press arrows on a device referred to as a dance pad in time with steps determined by the song's chart. The process of manual chart generation is timestaking and difficult, motivating interest in automation. We propose ITGPT, a new transformer based architecture for the generation of DDR/ITG charts, and demonstrate significant improvements to generation accuracy and computational cost in comparison to predecessor work.

</details>


### [446] [Can Tokens Compete? Token Representations against Supervised CNN Backbones for BirdCLEF+ 2026](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14474)
*Anthony Miyaguchi,Murilo Gustineli,Adrian Cheung*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper presents the DS@GT ARC team's approach to multi-label detection of animal sounds using audio analysis methods, focusing on BirdCLEF+ 2026 competition. 


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigating effective supervised pipelines for detecting animal vocalizations in labeled soundscapes, especially with an added challenge of a computational resource limit.

Method: The team implemented a supervised baseline with ensemble models that included Perch v2, HGNetV2-B0, and prototypical heads, as well as compared token-based encoding strategies utilizing bioacoustic and AudioSet-trained representations.

Result: The proposed model achieved a private leaderboard score of 0.936 at rank 1894 within a computational budget of 90 minutes of CPU usage.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates the effectiveness of ensemble supervised pipelines for detecting animal vocalizations and explores the potential of token-based representations in bioacoustic tasks.

Abstract: This paper details the DS@GT ARC team's approach to BirdCLEF+ 2026, multi-label detection of animal vocalizations in soundscapes from the Pantanal wetlands. The 2026 edition adds about an hour of labeled soundscapes, shifting the task toward supervised pipelines fit to the labeled set. First, we build a competitive supervised baseline that ensembles a frozen Perch v2 backbone, a trained HGNetV2-B0 sound-event-detection network, and a non-bird prototypical head, reaching a private leaderboard score of 0.936 at rank 1894 within a 90-minute CPU budget. Second, we ask whether token-based representations can compete, contrasting codec representations from neural audio codecs against semantic representations from foundational embeddings. We compare two bioacoustic specialist models against four token-based encoders trained on AudioSet. The repository for this work can be found at https://github.com/dsgt-arc/birdclef-2026.

</details>


### [447] [MIDI-RAE-JEPA: Hierarchical Representation Learning and Generation for Symbolic Music](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14537)
*Scott H. Hawley*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: MIDI-RAE-JEPA models symbolic music's hierarchical structures by leveraging equivariance objectives and a Swin Transformer to excel in downstream tasks and generative outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore self-supervised learning approaches for symbolic music representations that capture hierarchical and multiscale musical structures, enabling enhanced music understanding and applications.

Method: A pitch- and time-shift equivariance objective is combined with a Swin Transformer V2 encoder for self-supervised training. Masked embedding predictors and SIGReg are used to prevent collapse, and learned embeddings are employed for downstream tasks.

Result: The model achieves high reconstruction accuracy (F1 of 0.995), generates convincing music by conditioning on embeddings, outperforms a baseline on emotion classification, and shows measurable equivariance in embeddings.

Conclusion: Equivariance-based self-supervised learning objectives, when paired with sufficient encoder capacity, enable the development of semantically rich, generatively meaningful representations for symbolic music, beneficial for downstream tasks.

Abstract: Rich internal representations of musical structure are essential for music understanding tasks such as machine-assisted music co-writing, yet self-supervised approaches for symbolic music representation remain underexplored, particularly those that encode the hierarchical multiscale nature of musical structures. We present MIDI-RAE-JEPA, combining a pitch- and time-shift equivariance objective with LeJEPA and a Swin Transformer V2 encoder to learn such hierarchical representations of symbolic music encoded as piano roll images. The time-shift equivariance objective encourages the model to internalize temporal musical relationships. The encoder is trained purely on self-supervised objectives -- including a masked embedding predictor (MEP) -- with collapse prevented via SIGReg. A separate decoder trained on the frozen encoder embeddings achieves reconstruction F1 of 0.995, and a flow matching generative model conditioned on those embeddings produces generations that closely match the pitch register and rhythmic density of the conditioning excerpt, while mismatched conditioning yields unrelated but musically plausible output. Learned representations outperform a Haar scattering transform baseline on a downstream emotion classification task, and embedding distances increase monotonically with pitch and time shift magnitude, confirming measurable equivariance. These results suggest that equivariance-based SSL objectives, combined with sufficient fine-level encoder capacity, provide a viable path toward semantically rich, generatively useful representations of symbolic music.

</details>


### [448] [Large Audio Language Models for Spoofing-Aware Speaker Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14753)
*Sofya Savelyeva,Mariia Perunova,Evgeny Kushnir,Artem Dvirniak,Dmitrii Korzh,Oleg Y. Rogov*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: This paper evaluates large audio language models (LALMs) for spoofing-aware speaker verification (SASV) and compares them to traditional methods. LALMs show promise, but require adaptation to match performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the growing threat of voice spoofing to voice authentication systems, and the need to explore whether LALMs can effectively address spoofing-aware speaker verification.

Method: The paper evaluates LALMs under multiple settings: zero-shot prompting, supervised adaptation, reasoning-oriented training, and reinforcement learning, and compares them to traditional modular ASV-CM pipelines.

Result: LALMs performed poorly in zero-shot scenarios but improved significantly with task-specific adaptations, achieving competitive results through various approaches.

Conclusion: LALMs, though initially unsuited to SASV, can be adapted to perform effectively, offering unique benefits such as auditable predictions, while traditional systems still excel in certain areas.

Abstract: Recent advances in text-to-speech and voice cloning make high-quality spoofing inexpensive and scalable, threatening voice authentication systems, especially automatic speaker verification (ASV). Existing defenses mainly address this threat through binary countermeasures (CMs) for deepfake detection or spoofing-aware speaker verification (SASV), where current systems are dominated by modular ASV-CM fusion and cascaded pipelines. Although large audio language models (LALMs) have shown promise on related audio tasks, including CM and ASV, their use for SASV remains unexplored, despite their capacity to produce natural-language rationales for auditing and robustness beyond discriminative predictions. This work systematically evaluates LALMs for SASV against conventional pipelines under zero-shot prompting, supervised adaptation, reasoning-oriented training, and reinforcement-learning-based optimization. Our results show that pretrained LALMs are near chance in the zero-shot setting, confirming that they are not natively suited to SASV, but that task-specific adaptation closes this gap. We further find that competitive SASV performance can be achieved through several distinct routes. These findings position LALMs as a promising and auditable foundation for unified SASV, while clarifying where conventional cascade systems still lead.

</details>


### [449] [RW-Voice-EQ Bench: A Real World Benchmark for Evaluating Voice AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14846)
*David Ayllon,Alice Baird,Jeffrey Brooks,Franc Camps-Febrer,Jakub Piotr Cłapa,Theo Lebryk,Jens Madsen,Olya Ossipova,Sharath Rao,Hoon Shin,Tigran Soghbatyan,Georg Streich,Rashish Tandon,Panagiotis Tzirakis*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Real World Voice EQ Bench, a benchmark designed to evaluate voice AI systems comprehensively across various dimensions beyond traditional single-metric evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most current voice AI benchmarks focus on isolated metrics like speech intelligibility or word error rate, neglecting acoustic elements that differentiate spoken language from textual representations.

Method: The authors propose a multi-dimensional benchmark called Real World Voice EQ Bench, which assesses text-to-speech (TTS), speech-to-speech (STS), speech understanding (SU), and automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems under real-world acoustic, emotional, and conversational scenarios.

Result: The findings highlight that voice AI performance varies significantly across dimensions such as naturalness in TTS, utilization of vocal affect in STS, uneven performance in SU tasks, and vulnerability in ASR models under non-ideal conditions.

Conclusion: Voice AI systems should adopt a profile-based evaluation, encompassing acoustic, expressive, interactional, and robustness capacities, rather than relying on a single aggregate score.

Abstract: Current voice AI benchmarks typically evaluate isolated capabilities such as speech intelligibility, word error rate, or text-based dialogue quality, but they rarely test whether systems harness the acoustic information that distinguishes spoken language from its textual representation. To this end, we introduce the Real World Voice EQ Bench, a multidimensional benchmark for evaluating voice AI across text-to-speech (TTS), speech-to-speech (STS), speech understanding (SU), and automatic speech recognition (ASR). Our evaluations indicate that performance is highly dimension-specific. For TTS, naturalness, expressiveness, identity stability, and reliability are largely independent evaluation dimensions. For STS, access to audio does not guarantee use of vocal affect, and some agents remain largely transcript-driven. For SU, models perform unevenly across paralinguistic tasks. For ASR, real world accent, emotion, noise, and conversational conditions expose failures that are not captured by established clean-speech benchmarks. Together, these results show that voice AI should be evaluated as a profile of acoustic, expressive, interactional, and robustness capabilities rather than by a single aggregate score.

</details>


<div id='math.AP'></div>

# math.AP [[Back]](#toc)

### [450] [Riesz-Kernel Stein Variational Gradient Descent: Renormalized Entropy and Long-Time Particle Limits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14527)
*Trevor Teolis,Maarten V. de Hoop*

Main category: math.AP

TL;DR: The paper extends Stein variational gradient descent (SVGD) to use singular Riesz kernels for better convergence guarantees but resolves challenges linked to self-interaction in finite-particle systems, proving weak convergence to the target distribution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of singular Riesz kernels in Stein variational methods, particularly the issue of infinite self-interaction at the finite-particle level, while leveraging their strong convergence properties.

Method: The authors analyze periodic Riesz SVGD with self-interaction removed and employ mathematical proofs to demonstrate sampling convergence properties under conditions such as local integrability of Stein energy and finite relative entropy.

Result: The study proves weak convergence of the empirical measure to a target distribution under certain bounded entropy conditions, provides explicit finite-particle error bounds below logarithmic singularity, and extends existing smooth-kernel SVGD methodologies to singular interactions.

Conclusion: Removing self-interaction in singular Riesz kernel SVGD allows particles to achieve theoretical convergence guarantees, demonstrating the practicality and robustness of this approach for sampling from target distributions.

Abstract: Stein variational gradient descent (SVGD) transports interacting particles toward a target distribution through deterministic kernelized dynamics. Singular Riesz kernels are attractive because they can provide quantitative population-level convergence, but at the finite-particle level the corresponding Stein energy has infinite self-interaction. We study periodic Riesz SVGD with self-interaction removed and prove a many-particle, long-time sampling theorem. Throughout the range in which the singular Stein energy is locally integrable, under a uniform bound on the initial relative entropy per particle, the time-averaged empirical-measure law converges weakly to the point mass \(δ_π\) at the target as the particle number and any diverging averaging horizon tend to infinity. We also show that the empirical-measure laws induced by invariant particle laws of finite relative entropy converge weakly to \(δ_π\), without a uniform entropy bound. Below the logarithmic singularity threshold, we obtain an explicit algebraic finite-particle error bound. These results extend the joint-entropy approach for smooth-kernel SVGD to singular interactions.

</details>


<div id='math.DS'></div>

# math.DS [[Back]](#toc)

### [451] [The Planar Case of Thomas Positive Circuits Conjecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14143)
*Natan Katz*

Main category: math.DS

TL;DR: This paper explores R. Thomas's conjecture on the necessity of positive circuits for multi-stationarity in planar systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate R. Thomas's conjecture linking positive circuits to multi-stationarity in dynamical systems, focusing on planar systems.

Method: The study employs tools of dynamical systems and planar analysis to derive conditions validating the conjecture for planar systems.

Result: The analysis identifies specific conditions under which positive circuits are necessary for multi-stationarity in planar systems.

Conclusion: The paper supports R. Thomas's conjecture by outlining conditions where positive circuits enable multi-stationarity in planar systems.

Abstract: The notion of circuit refers to a cyclic oriented influence between the elements of a dynamical system. There are two classes of circuit: positive and negative. R. Thomas conjectured that a necessary condition of multi stationarity is the existence of positive circuits. In this paper we use dynamical system tools and planar analysis to find conditions for which the conjecture holds for planar systems.

</details>


<div id='eess.SY'></div>

# eess.SY [[Back]](#toc)

### [452] [Information-Theoretic Adaptive Cooling for Deterministic MPPI via Entropy Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14245)
*Shuqi Wang,Wenrong Sun,Tao Han,Yue Gao,Xiang Yin*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The paper introduces an Information-Theoretic Adaptive Cooling (ITAC) framework for Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) control, enhancing sampling efficiency and convergence rates with an adaptive temperature regulation method.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deterministic MPPI methods use predefined cooling schedules, limiting efficiency and robustness in systems with complex dynamics and nonsmooth objectives.

Method: ITAC leverages Shannon entropy of importance weights as an online feedback signal for dynamically regulating temperature during control optimization.

Result: ITAC improves sampling efficiency and achieves faster convergence in nonsmooth motion-planning tasks compared to state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: The ITAC framework provides a robust alternative to traditional cooling approaches in deterministic MPPI, ensuring efficient sampling and convergence while maintaining derivative-free optimization.

Abstract: This paper investigates deterministic optimal control using Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) control, a sampling-based and derivative-free framework well suited for systems with complex dynamics and nonsmooth objectives. In deterministic MPPI, the temperature must be driven to zero to recover the true optimum, yet the design of an effective cooling schedule remains a fundamental challenge. Existing methods typically rely on predefined open-loop schedules, which limit the efficiency and robustness of the algorithm. To overcome this limitation, we propose an Information-Theoretic Adaptive Cooling (ITAC) framework that uses the Shannon entropy of the importance weights as an online feedback signal to regulate the temperature. The proposed mechanism adapts the cooling rate to the current sampling state, enabling fast progress when the weights are diffuse and cautious cooling when they become concentrated. We prove asymptotic convergence of the resulting scheme to the deterministic optimum, and further derive a critical entropy threshold that leads to a smooth barrier against premature weight collapse. Experiments on nonsmooth signal temporal logic motion-planning tasks show that ITAC improves sampling efficiency and achieves substantially faster convergence than state-of-the-art baselines without sacrificing the derivative-free nature of MPPI.

</details>


<div id='quant-ph'></div>

# quant-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [453] [Real-Time Detection of Charge Jumps in Superconducting Qubits with a Convolutional Neural Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14293)
*Daniel Gaytan-Villarreal,Peter Meiring,Daniel Baxter,Daniel Bowring,Grace Bratrud,Matteo Cremonesi,Giuseppe Di Guglielmo,Grace Wagner,Bowen Xiao*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: This paper introduces an online detector for charge jumps in superconducting qubits using a convolutional neural network, achieving real-time detection with FPGA implementation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Charge jumps induced by cosmic rays and gamma radiation cause errors in superconducting qubits, creating challenges for quantum computing and opportunities for particle detection. A real-time detection method is needed for adaptive control.

Method: A dilated causal convolutional neural network (DCCNN) trained on synthetic Ramsey tomography data and implemented on the QICK platform using FPGA firmware via hls4ml was developed for online charge-jump detection.

Result: The DCCNN achieves a detection efficiency comparable to traditional offline methods (≈84%) with a low per-inference latency (6.19 μs) and requires no per-qubit hyperparameter tuning.

Conclusion: The proposed method enables real-time detection of charge jumps, shifting it into a feedback control loop and enhancing applications in quantum error mitigation and quantum sensing.

Abstract: Ionizing radiation from cosmic rays and gammas can induce discontinuous jumps in the environmental charge of superconducting qubits (charge jumps), causing correlated errors that challenge fault-tolerant quantum computing while simultaneously providing a detection signature for quantum sensing applications. Current detection methods operate offline, introducing latency incompatible with in-the-loop qubit control. In this paper, an online detector of charge jumps for superconducting qubits, based on a dilated causal convolutional neural network (DCCNN) designed for in-the-loop deployment on the Quantum Instrumentation Control Kit (QICK) platform, is presented. The network is trained on synthetic Ramsey tomography scans generated from qubit templates measured at the Northwestern Experimental Underground Site (NEXUS) at Fermilab, and translated to FPGA firmware via hls4ml with ap_fixed$\langle 16,6 \rangle$ quantization, reaching a per-inference latency of $6.19 μ$s on the Zynq UltraScale+ RFSoC ZCU216.
